questions.stem: A 34-year-old Asian woman attends preconception clinic. She weighs 98 kg, BMI 35, has type 1 diabetes mellitus on insulin. Recent blood sugar levels are within normal limits. HbA1c is 5.2%.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Moderate to severe pulmonary hypertension carries a maternal mortality of 30–50% in pregnancy. Pregnancy is absolutely contraindicated. The woman should be strongly advised against pregnancy and referred to a specialist centre. This is a clear indication for advice against pregnancy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: advice regarding diet, nutrition and exercise

choices.label: C
choices.text: cessation of smoking and lifestyle modification

choices.label: D
choices.text: complete blood count, HbA1c, blood sugar and renal function tests

choices.label: E
choices.text: complete blood count, screening for asymptomatic bacteriuria

choices.label: G
choices.text: lifestyle advice and high-dose folic acid supplement
choices.explanation: In a diabetic woman with good glycaemic control (HbA1c <6.5%), high-dose folic acid (5 mg/day) is recommended preconceptionally due to neural tube defect risk. Lifestyle advice regarding her obesity (BMI 35) is also essential. Standard folic acid dose is 400 mcg for general population, but 5 mg for diabetics, epileptics and BMI >30.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: A woman with a single kidney who is otherwise healthy and has normal renal function can conceive. She needs preconceptional folic acid 400 mcg/day. Rubella immunity is confirmed, so no further rubella screening is needed. There are no specific high-risk features requiring high-dose folic acid.

choices.label: M
choices.text: rubella screening and 400 mcg folic acid daily
choices.explanation: Clozapine is an atypical antipsychotic associated with agranulocytosis and metabolic complications. It requires regular blood monitoring. While psychiatric conditions do not automatically preclude pregnancy, the patient needs multidisciplinary preconception counselling. The standard rubella screening and folic acid advice applies; the psychiatric team needs to review clozapine safety in pregnancy. Answer M (rubella screening and 400 mcg folic acid) is the first-line preconception advice.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old presents to A&E with fever, vomiting, severe abdominal/flank pain. LMP 6 weeks ago. Positive UPT, urinalysis shows pus cells and epithelial cells. Dehydrated, BP 80/40 mmHg, PR 98 bpm, rigidity and guarding in pelvic region.

choices.label: B
choices.text: commence low-molecular-weight heparin and low-dose aspirin

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: This is urinary retention — a recognised complication of a retroverted gravid uterus (incarceration of uterus) in early pregnancy, especially around 14 weeks. The treatment is immediate catheterisation and reassurance. This relieves the obstruction and usually allows the uterus to spontaneously antevert.

choices.label: D
choices.text: inpatient HDU care with hydration, analgesia and observation

choices.label: F
choices.text: intramuscular methotrexate followed by folinic acid

choices.label: G
choices.text: intravenous access and urgent pelvic ultrasonography
choices.explanation: Heterotopic pregnancy (simultaneous intrauterine and ectopic pregnancy) is a rare but serious condition. Even though the ectopic sac has no cardiac activity, this requires urgent evaluation with IV access and urgent pelvic ultrasonography to plan management. The ectopic component must be treated (usually laparoscopic salpingectomy or systemic methotrexate carefully), while preserving the intrauterine pregnancy.

choices.label: I
choices.text: MSU culture followed by intravenous antibiotics
choices.explanation: This picture is consistent with severe pyelonephritis in early pregnancy (urosepsis) with positive urine for pus cells, fever, flank pain, and haemodynamic compromise. Urgent MSU culture and IV antibiotics are needed. The septic picture with rigidity/guarding also warrants urgent pelvic USS to exclude ectopic, but the primary next step is MSU + IV antibiotics given the urinary findings and sepsis.

choices.label: J
choices.text: offer detailed counselling about selective termination
choices.explanation: Spontaneous quadruplet pregnancy carries extreme risks including preterm birth, growth restriction, and maternal morbidity. Detailed counselling about selective fetal reduction should be offered. The mild dysuria should be investigated (MSU) but the primary management decision in quadruplet pregnancy is selective termination counselling. J is the correct answer.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old at 30 weeks gestation presents with fever, flank pain and palpitations. Febrile, PR 100 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg. Diffuse tenderness over abdomen, soft non-tender uterus. Vaginal exam normal. Fetal heart rate 200 bpm.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: First episode (primary) genital herpes at term (or within 6 weeks of delivery) is an indication for Caesarean section (category 3 LSCS) to prevent neonatal herpes, which carries high mortality/morbidity. This is different from recurrent herpes near term where the risk is much lower. RCOG recommends CS for primary HSV within 6 weeks of expected delivery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: combined vaccination and immunisation (active and passive)
choices.explanation: PPROM at 32 weeks in a Hepatitis B surface antigen positive mother. The baby needs combined active and passive immunisation (HBV vaccine + HBIG) at birth to prevent vertical transmission. Immediate management of PPROM at 32 weeks also includes betamethasone and erythromycin, but the specific Hepatitis B management requires combined vaccination and immunisation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: high dependency care with intravenous antibiotics and hydration
choices.explanation: The picture is severe pyelonephritis in pregnancy with sepsis and fetal tachycardia (fetal heart rate 200 bpm indicates fetal compromise from maternal fever/sepsis). This requires high-dependency care with IV antibiotics and hydration. The fetal tachycardia should resolve as the maternal infection is treated.

choices.label: J
choices.text: oral acyclovir 400 mg three times daily for 5 days
choices.explanation: Shallow painful perineal ulcers suggest primary genital herpes simplex (HSV) infection. At 12 weeks, urinary retention can occur due to extensive herpetic lesions causing pain on voiding. Treatment is oral acyclovir 400 mg three times daily for 5 days for primary HSV. Catheterisation may also be needed for the urinary retention.

choices.label: K
choices.text: oral acyclovir 800 mg five times daily for 7 days

choices.label: M
choices.text: serum antibody testing varicella IgG and IgM

choices.label: N
choices.text: urine routine analysis and midstream culture

questions.stem: A 26-year-old Greek woman at 8 weeks gestation. Has sickle cell disease (HbSS), partner from same ethnic background with HbSC.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Echogenic bowel is a soft marker for cystic fibrosis (CF), and both parents are CF mutation carriers. There is a 25% chance the fetus has CF. Amniocentesis and FISH/PCR testing of fetal DNA is indicated to diagnose fetal CF. Amniocentesis is preferred at 20 weeks over CVS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: blood sugar levels fasting and postprandial, HbA1C levels

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: With HbSS mother and HbSC father, there is a 50% chance the fetus will have a clinically significant haemoglobinopathy (HbSC or HbSS). Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) at 10-14 weeks allows earlier fetal DNA analysis for haemoglobin status than amniocentesis, enabling earlier decision-making.

choices.label: G

questions.stem: A 24-year-old primigravida: target scan at 21 weeks shows bilateral choroid plexus cysts. No other problems. First trimester combined screening is negative.

choices.label: B
choices.text: blood sugar levels fasting and postprandial, HbA1C levels

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A nuchal translucency (NT) ≥3.5 mm is significantly elevated and associated with chromosomal abnormalities (trisomy 21, 18, 13), structural abnormalities, and genetic syndromes. Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) is recommended for karyotyping. The history of two mid-trimester losses adds further concern. CVS is preferred at 12 weeks.

choices.label: F
choices.text: maternal blood test of fetal cells

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Mild pyelectasis (renal pelvis dilatation 5-10 mm in the second trimester) is a common finding. With isolated mild bilateral pyelectasis (both <10 mm), no other soft markers, and normal parameters, the management is postnatal renal ultrasound to assess for pelviureteric junction obstruction or vesicoureteric reflux. Postnatal USS at 4-6 weeks is recommended.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Isolated choroid plexus cysts (CPCs) in the context of a low-risk combined screening test carry no significant increased risk for chromosomal abnormality (particularly trisomy 18). When isolated and with a low-risk first trimester screen, reassurance is appropriate without further invasive testing.

choices.label: M
choices.explanation: Isolated short femur length (below 3rd centile) at the anatomy scan, with a normal first trimester screening, is a soft marker that may indicate skeletal dysplasia or IUGR. Serial growth scans in the third trimester are recommended to monitor fetal growth and exclude late-onset IUGR. Genetic causes should be considered if severe.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old second gravida presents at 26 weeks with leaking vaginal. No pain or bleeding. Soft non-tender uterus, no cervical changes. Pooling of liquor in posterior fornix. Normal antenatal period and anomaly scan.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Preterm labour at 33 weeks with regular contractions and cervical changes. The priority is betamethasone (12 mg IM two doses 24 hours apart) for fetal lung maturity as delivery appears imminent. Tocolysis is unlikely to prevent delivery but may allow time for corticosteroids to act. Betamethasone is the single most important intervention.

choices.label: B
choices.text: category I lower segment Caesarean section
choices.explanation: Tense, board-like uterus with painful contractions and bleeding at 35 weeks is classic for placental abruption. A tense uterus with non-relaxing contractions and haemorrhage suggests severe abruption, which is an indication for immediate delivery. Category I LSCS is required, especially if the fetal heart is compromised or cervix is unfavourable.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: At 23 weeks gestation, a cervical length of ≤25 mm (1.5 cm) in a symptomatic patient with no active labour or PPROM raises concern for cervical incompetence and threatened preterm birth. Emergency cervical cerclage can be offered if cervical dilatation is <4 cm and membranes are intact, to prolong pregnancy. RCOG supports rescue cerclage in this scenario.

choices.label: G
choices.text: high vaginal swab for bacterial culture

choices.label: I
choices.text: indomethacin 100 mg per rectal once 24 hours

choices.label: J
choices.text: intravenous Atosiban 6.75 mg over 1-minute bolus followed by infusion

choices.label: L
choices.text: MSU culture and urine routine analysis

choices.label: M
choices.explanation: This is PPROM (preterm prelabour rupture of membranes) at 26 weeks. RCOG guidelines recommend oral erythromycin (250 mg four times daily for 10 days or until in established labour) to reduce infection-related morbidity, plus a course of betamethasone for fetal lung maturity. This combination (M) is the first-line management for PPROM before labour.

choices.label: N
choices.text: oral nifedipine 20 mg followed by 10-20 mg four doses in 24 hours

questions.stem: A 34-year-old collapses in day assessment unit. Heavy bleeding since morning. Discharged previous day after SVD. Unconscious, BP 60/40 mmHg, weak PR, respiratory rate 36/min.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Uterine atony during laparotomy not responding adequately to uterotonics, with the patient haemodynamically stable, allows time for surgical intervention. B-Lynch brace suture (compression suture) is the next step — it compresses the uterus and reduces bleeding. It is used before proceeding to hysterectomy to preserve the uterus.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Well-contracted uterus with profuse vaginal bleeding after delivery — the cause is NOT uterine atony. A well-contracted uterus with significant bleeding suggests genital tract trauma (cervical/vaginal lacerations), which requires examination under anaesthesia (EUA) to identify and repair the source of bleeding.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Foley's catheter, maternal observation including output
choices.explanation: The uterus is responding to oxytocin and there is no atony emergency. However, monitoring is essential. Foley's catheter (for urine output monitoring) and maternal observations (BP, PR, urine output) should be instituted. The uterus is responding to oxytocin, so escalating to surgical management is premature. E is correct.

choices.label: K
choices.text: manual removal of placenta under general anaesthesia

choices.label: N
choices.text: resuscitation with two wide-bore cannula, CBC, coagulation profile, U&Es and cross-match 6 units

choices.label: O
choices.explanation: The immediate priority in an unconscious patient with haemorrhagic shock is securing the airway and 100% oxygen (ABCDE approach). Before any definitive haemorrhage control, the airway must be secured. This is the primary step in any collapsed patient — O (secure airway and 100% oxygen).

questions.stem: A 30-year-old multiparous woman: precipitate labour and quick delivery in A&E. Atonic uterus, deep perineal tear, profuse vaginal bleeding. IV access secured, blood products arranged.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Uterine atony during laparotomy not responding adequately to uterotonics, with the patient haemodynamically stable, allows time for surgical intervention. B-Lynch brace suture (compression suture) is the next step — it compresses the uterus and reduces bleeding. It is used before proceeding to hysterectomy to preserve the uterus.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Well-contracted uterus with profuse vaginal bleeding after delivery — the cause is NOT uterine atony. A well-contracted uterus with significant bleeding suggests genital tract trauma (cervical/vaginal lacerations), which requires examination under anaesthesia (EUA) to identify and repair the source of bleeding.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Foley's catheter, maternal observation including output
choices.explanation: The uterus is responding to oxytocin and there is no atony emergency. However, monitoring is essential. Foley's catheter (for urine output monitoring) and maternal observations (BP, PR, urine output) should be instituted. The uterus is responding to oxytocin, so escalating to surgical management is premature. E is correct.

choices.label: K
choices.text: manual removal of placenta under general anaesthesia

choices.label: N
choices.text: resuscitation with two wide-bore cannula, CBC, coagulation profile, U&Es and cross-match 6 units

choices.label: P
choices.explanation: Atonic uterus is the most common cause of PPH. The first medical step after IV access is syntocinon (oxytocin) infusion — 40 units in 500 mL. This is the first-line uterotonic agent. The perineal tear also needs repair, but uterine atony must be addressed first with uterotonics.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old elective CS for IUGR with previous CS. After fetal delivery, uterus tonic but placenta morbidly adherent with no clear plane of separation. Placenta accreta suspected on earlier scan.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: PPH not controlled with oxytocin and misoprostol in a pre-eclampsia patient on magnesium sulfate. Ergometrine is relatively contraindicated in hypertension/pre-eclampsia (causes vasoconstriction and raises BP). The next step is intramuscular carboprost (Haemabate) 0.25 mg — a prostaglandin F2α analogue that causes uterine contraction. H is correct.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Confirmed placenta accreta with no plane of cleavage during CS means manual removal will cause catastrophic haemorrhage. The management is laparotomy/hysterectomy (peripartum hysterectomy). All prerequisites were arranged preoperatively. Hysterectomy (K) is the correct definitive management for placenta accreta when there is no plane.

choices.label: P
choices.text: two wide-bore cannula, CBC, coagulation profile, U&Es and cross-match 6 units
choices.explanation: This is uterine inversion with haemodynamic shock. The uterus has been replaced. Now with atonic uterus and haemodynamic instability, immediate resuscitation is the priority: two wide-bore cannulas, CBC, coagulation profile, cross-match 6 units of blood, and IV fluids. P is correct as the immediate resuscitative step.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old third gravida at 38 weeks presents with leaking vaginal. Mild contractions, fetal heart 140 bpm. Vaginal examination shows a soft pulsatile mass in the vagina.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: A big mass at the introitus after delivery = acute uterine inversion. The mother has collapsed. Immediate action is to call for help (A) — this is a life-threatening obstetric emergency requiring immediate multidisciplinary response: anaesthetist, senior obstetrician, blood bank. Then L (ABCDE resuscitation) follows.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sudden profound fetal bradycardia in a patient with previous CS (risk of uterine scar rupture) — this is a category I emergency. Vertex at -3 station means delivery is not imminent vaginally. With suspected uterine scar dehiscence or acute fetal compromise, Category I LSCS is required immediately.

choices.label: I
choices.text: internal podalic version followed by breech extraction

choices.label: L
choices.text: secure airway, oxygen breathing and circulation
choices.explanation: Eclamptic seizure in a pre-eclamptic patient. The immediate response is ABCDE (L — secure airway, oxygen, breathing and circulation) while simultaneously calling for help. After securing the airway and oxygen, intravenous magnesium sulfate is given to terminate the seizure and prevent further fits. L is the immediate first step.

choices.label: M
choices.text: start oxytocin infusion 5 units in 500 mL of Hartman's solution

choices.label: O
choices.explanation: A soft pulsatile mass in the vagina with leaking liquor is cord prolapse. Immediate management includes: (1) Call for help, (2) Trendelenburg/knee-chest position + bladder filling (O) to relieve pressure on the cord by elevating the presenting part, (3) Category I CS. Bladder filling with 500 mL saline elevates the fetal head off the cord, maintaining fetal oxygenation while preparing for emergency CS.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old lady, induced at 10 days post-term, forceps delivery under epidural. 30 seconds after delivery of the head, difficulty is encountered.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 30 seconds after delivery of the head with difficulty in completing delivery = shoulder dystocia. The HELPERR protocol begins immediately. The FIRST step is calling for help (A). Then McRoberts manoeuvre + suprapubic pressure are applied simultaneously, followed by internal manoeuvres.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old collapsed 6 days after normal delivery. Previously uneventful hospital course. Feeble pulse but no recordable BP.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Sudden chest pain and breathing difficulty during labour = suspect amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) or pulmonary embolism. The FIRST response is always calling for help (A), activating the maternal collapse protocol. Then ABCDE assessment, airway protection, oxygen, resuscitation. AFE is a catastrophic event requiring immediate multidisciplinary team activation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: high dependency unit admission, hydration, supportive care
choices.explanation: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) in pregnancy — confusion, tachycardia, tachypnoea, ketonuria. Management requires HDU admission with IV insulin (sliding scale) and hydration. However, the correct answer here is E (HDU care with hydration, supportive care) as the question asks about initial management; H (insulin sliding scale) is the specific diabetic treatment but E is the immediate management frame.

choices.label: F
choices.text: initial stabilisation and Category 1 Caesarean section
choices.explanation: Rigid uterus with severe abdominal pain, fetal bradycardia, haemodynamic collapse in a smoker — this is severe placental abruption with maternal haemodynamic instability and fetal compromise. After initial stabilisation, Category 1 LSCS is required immediately. Waiting for further investigations is not appropriate; F (initial stabilisation + Cat 1 CS) is correct.

choices.label: M
choices.text: secure airway and 100% oxygen, and intravenous access
choices.explanation: Collapsed postnatal woman with haemodynamic instability — ABCDE approach first. Secure airway, 100% oxygen and intravenous access (M) is the immediate priority. The cause could be PPH, pulmonary embolism, sepsis or eclampsia. Initial stabilisation with airway, oxygen and IV access precedes all other investigations.

choices.label: N
choices.text: two wide-bore cannula, CBC, coagulation profile U&Es and cross-match

questions.stem: An 18-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks, history of substance abuse, suddenly collapses at GP surgery. Initial resuscitation done, being transported to hospital.

choices.label: A
choices.text: ABC — check airway, breathing and oxygen, and intravenous access
choices.explanation: Sudden collapse with chest tightness during amniocentesis — this suggests anaphylaxis, vasovagal episode, or amniotic fluid embolism. The immediate response is ABCDE: check airway, breathing and oxygen, and IV access (A). Resuscitation must begin immediately. This is the primary response before calling for help.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ST elevation on ECG with chest pain, haemodynamic compromise — this is ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). In a stable patient, coronary angiography (C) with possible primary PCI is the next step in management. Pregnancy/postnatal state does not change STEMI management — angiography and revascularisation are indicated.

choices.label: F
choices.text: high dependency unit admission, hydration, supportive care
choices.explanation: Collapse in a pregnant substance abuser — causes include opioid overdose, cardiac arrhythmia, or other drug effects. After initial resuscitation by the GP, HDU admission, hydration and supportive care (F) is required. The specific cause will be established in hospital. This is an HDU level scenario requiring supportive care while the cause is evaluated.

Pregnancy/postnatal state does not change STEMI management — angiography and revascularisation are indicated.

choices.label: F
choices.text: high dependency unit admission, hydration, supportive care
choices.explanation: Collapse in a pregnant substance abuser — causes include opioid overdose, cardiac arrhythmia, or other drug effects. After initial resuscitation by the GP, HDU admission, hydration and supportive care (F) is required. The specific cause will be established in hospital. This is an HDU level scenario requiring supportive care while the cause is evaluated.

choices.label: G
choices.text: initial stabilization and lower segment Caesarean section

choices.label: J
choices.text: intravenous heparin loading dose followed by infusion

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Focal neurological deficit (unilateral arm weakness and numbness) in a patient with a prosthetic heart valve suggests embolic stroke (cardioembolism from prosthetic valve). MRI brain (L) is indicated for stroke evaluation. The patient is on thromboprophylaxis but this may be insufficient.

choices.label: N
choices.text: two wide-bore cannula, CBC, coagulation profile U&Es and cross-match

questions.stem: An 18-year-old third gravida with no live births: frequent urinary infections, 12 weeks pregnant. Antibiotics prescribed, normal USS. Culture always negative.

choices.label: A
choices.text: arranging a translator to be in attendance

choices.label: B
choices.text: call for help and start basic life support

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Recurrent urinary complaints with consistently negative cultures in a young woman with no live births despite multiple pregnancies suggests coercive sexual activity or domestic abuse (somatoform/functional symptoms from abuse). Counselling is the appropriate first-line approach — to establish trust and explore domestic violence, safeguarding and support needs.

choices.label: G
choices.text: enquiring and training the staff concerned

choices.label: J
choices.text: involvement of senior clinician at delivery for anterior episiotomy
choices.explanation: Extensive labial adhesions at 36 weeks gestation suggest type III FGM (infibulation). This may obstruct labour and delivery significantly. Involvement of a senior clinician at delivery for anterior episiotomy/defibulation is required for safe delivery (J). The FGM should also be documented and referred to FGM specialist midwife.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Multiple bruises all over the body, repeated presentations with vague symptoms, and a history of fall in a pregnant woman is strongly suggestive of domestic violence. The appropriate response is to liaise with social services (K) for safeguarding both the mother and the unborn baby.

choices.label: L
choices.text: postexposure prophylaxis for HIV and continued sexual health follow-up

choices.label: N
choices.explanation: Extensive genital herpetic lesions at 28 weeks require specialist management through the GUM clinic (N) for proper STI assessment, treatment, counselling regarding neonatal HSV risk, and multidisciplinary antenatal care planning.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 16 weeks: chest pain and breathlessness. Smokes 15 cigarettes/day, not yet seen midwife. Very pale, dyspnoeic, PR 112 bpm, RR 30/min. Clinically stable, admitted to HDU.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Extensive iliac vein thrombosis not controlled with therapeutic LMWH — cava filters (inferior vena cava filters) are indicated in cases of recurrent DVT despite therapeutic anticoagulation, or when anticoagulation is contraindicated, especially with iliac vein extension. IVC filter (A) prevents pulmonary embolism.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Warfarin is teratogenic (causes warfarin embryopathy) in the first trimester (weeks 6-12). A woman on warfarin who is pregnant should be changed to LMWH (low-molecular-weight heparin) as soon as pregnancy is confirmed. B (change to LMWH) is correct.

choices.label: C
choices.text: commence LMWH enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg body weight
choices.explanation: Clinically stable with features of pulmonary embolism (chest pain, breathlessness, tachycardia, tachypnoea) in a pregnant woman with risk factors (smoking, no booking). Treatment-dose LMWH should be started — enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg body weight once daily (therapeutic dose) for confirmed/high-suspicion PE. CTPA would confirm diagnosis but treatment should not be delayed.

choices.label: K
choices.text: initial resuscitation, FBC, coagulation profile and LFTs

choices.label: N
choices.text: prophylactic LMWH enoxaparin 40 mg once daily
choices.explanation: Post-Caesarean section thromboprophylaxis — prophylactic LMWH is indicated. For a patient weighing 78 kg without other high-risk features, enoxaparin 40 mg once daily subcutaneously is the standard prophylactic dose (N). This should be started 6 hours after CS if haemostasis is adequate.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old presents to A&E with generalised tonic-clonic convulsions, becomes unconscious. Ultrasound shows a live 10-week intrauterine pregnancy.

choices.label: A
choices.text: admission to HDU and supportive care

choices.label: B
choices.text: airway, face mask oxygen and intravenous access
choices.explanation: Eclampsia at 10 weeks is rare but possible. The immediate priority in a convulsing patient is airway, face mask oxygen and intravenous access (B) — basic life support first, then magnesium sulfate to control/prevent further seizures. The airway must be secured before any medication is given.

choices.label: D
choices.text: blood investigation CBC, urea and electrolytes, creatinine, LFT and uric acid
choices.explanation: New-onset hypertension with proteinuria in labour at 37 weeks = pre-eclampsia. Blood investigations (D) — CBC, U&E, creatinine, LFT, uric acid — are needed urgently to assess severity and end-organ damage before deciding on further management. Baseline bloods are essential in all newly diagnosed pre-eclampsia.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Hypertension (BP ≥140/90) at 18 weeks without proteinuria suggests chronic/gestational hypertension. Treatment should be commenced when BP is consistently ≥150/100 or ≥140/90 with symptoms. Oral labetalol is first-line antihypertensive in pregnancy (G). ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated.

choices.label: K
choices.text: intravenous hydralazine 10 mg bolus followed by maintenance

choices.label: L
choices.text: intravenous magnesium sulphate 4 g bolus followed by 1 g/hour
choices.explanation: Severe pre-eclampsia with BP 160/110. The patient is in HDU with catheter. The next priority is IV magnesium sulphate for seizure prophylaxis (L) — 4 g bolus over 15-20 minutes, then 1 g/hour maintenance. Also requires antihypertensive treatment (IV labetalol or hydralazine). Magnesium is the first-line for seizure prevention in severe pre-eclampsia.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks: reduced fetal movements 2 days. Normal USS except marked oligohydramnios and normal Dopplers. Bishop score 5. Membrane sweep done.

choices.label: D
choices.text: cervical stretch and sweep of membranes
choices.explanation: At 37 weeks with Bishop score 5 for an elective induction, the next step is cervical stretch and sweep (D) to further ripen the cervix before formal induction at 38 weeks. This is preparatory and appropriate in a multiparous woman with twins approaching the planned induction date.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A woman requesting Caesarean section for polyhydramnios with no indication (normal Dopplers, no anomalies, good fetal movements) — previous miscarriages in first trimester do not constitute an indication for CS. Counselling (E) is appropriate: explaining that polyhydramnios with normal parameters does not necessitate CS, and discussing the risks/benefits of CS vs induction.

choices.label: J
choices.text: oxytocin infusion with continuous fetal heart monitoring

choices.label: N
choices.explanation: Oligohydramnios at 38 weeks with Bishop score 5 — induction is indicated. With Bishop score 5 (unfavourable cervix, <6), cervical ripening with vaginal PGE2 gel (N) is appropriate. With a Bishop score of 5, amniotomy alone may not be feasible; PGE2 gel is used to ripen the cervix before amniotomy.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old with 2 previous normal deliveries at 37 weeks: DCDA twins, both cephalic, Bishop score 5. Elective induction due at 38 weeks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: cervical stretch and sweep of membranes
choices.explanation: At 37 weeks with Bishop score 5 for an elective induction, the next step is cervical stretch and sweep (D) to further ripen the cervix before formal induction at 38 weeks. This is preparatory and appropriate in a multiparous woman with twins approaching the planned induction date.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A woman requesting Caesarean section for polyhydramnios with no indication (normal Dopplers, no anomalies, good fetal movements) — previous miscarriages in first trimester do not constitute an indication for CS. Counselling (E) is appropriate: explaining that polyhydramnios with normal parameters does not necessitate CS, and discussing the risks/benefits of CS vs induction.

choices.label: J
choices.text: oxytocin infusion with continuous fetal heart monitoring

choices.label: N
choices.explanation: Post-term (term +10) with unfavourable cervix (Bishop score 3) after a failed sweep — induction with vaginal PGE2 gel (N) is indicated. PGE2 gel ripens the cervix and induces contractions. Misoprostol is also an option but PGE2 is the standard first-line agent in the UK.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at term +10. BMI 28, normal fetal movements, normal admission CTG. Bishop score 3, previously had stretch and sweep.

choices.label: D
choices.text: cervical stretch and sweep of membranes
choices.explanation: At 37 weeks with Bishop score 5 for an elective induction, the next step is cervical stretch and sweep (D) to further ripen the cervix before formal induction at 38 weeks. This is preparatory and appropriate in a multiparous woman with twins approaching the planned induction date.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: A woman requesting Caesarean section for polyhydramnios with no indication (normal Dopplers, no anomalies, good fetal movements) — previous miscarriages in first trimester do not constitute an indication for CS. Counselling (E) is appropriate: explaining that polyhydramnios with normal parameters does not necessitate CS, and discussing the risks/benefits of CS vs induction.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 39 weeks: regular uterine contractions, leaking vaginal 4 hours. 4 cm dilated, well-effaced cervix, vertex at -3 station. Clear liquor.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Secondary arrest of labour with thick oedematous cervix, marked caput and moulding indicates cephalopelvic disproportion (CPD). In a multiparous woman with secondary arrest despite adequate contractions, failed progress with significant caput/moulding — Caesarean section (B) is indicated. This is NOT a case for oxytocin augmentation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: continuous fetal heart monitoring and expectant management
choices.explanation: Slow progress from 4 cm to 6 cm in 4 hours in a primigravida on oxytocin with a fundal fibroid — this is protracted active phase. The fibroid is fundal (not obstructive). With continuous fetal heart monitoring reassuring and some progress, expectant management with continuous CTG (C) is appropriate before considering CS.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A 4 cm dilated primigravida in established labour with intact/ruptured membranes, vertex presentation, reassuring fetal state, and no risk factors — standard management is intermittent fetal auscultation (G) during labour, as per NICE guidelines for low-risk women. Continuous CTG is not routinely indicated in uncomplicated labour.

choices.label: K
choices.text: oxytocin infusion with continuous fetal heart monitoring
choices.explanation: After delivery of the first twin, the second twin is in longitudinal lie with head at -2 station and a reassuring CTG. Standard management is to start oxytocin infusion to maintain contractions (K) and aim for delivery of the second twin within 30 minutes with continuous CTG monitoring.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old primigravida with MCDA twins at 20 weeks. Both structurally normal on anomaly scan, no abnormalities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: betamethasone 12 mg two doses 12 hours apart
choices.explanation: At 32 weeks with preterm contractions (tightenings) in a twin pregnancy with previous CS — betamethasone (12 mg IM two doses 12 hours apart) is indicated for fetal lung maturity in case of imminent preterm delivery. Previous CS means VBAC considerations also apply.

choices.label: D
choices.text: consultant-led antenatal care in fetal medicine unit
choices.explanation: Triplet pregnancy that has declined selective fetal reduction — these women require consultant-led antenatal care in a fetal medicine unit (D) with intensive surveillance for complications including TTTS (if monochorionic), IUGR, preterm labour, and maternal complications.

choices.label: I
choices.text: inpatient care and steroids (betamethasone 12 mg two doses)

choices.label: O
choices.explanation: MCDA (monochorionic diamniotic) twins are at risk of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) and selective IUGR due to shared placentation. Management includes serial growth scans every 2-3 weeks and fortnightly Doppler studies from 16 weeks. M (reassurance alone) is insufficient; serial USS is mandatory for MCDA twins.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old second gravida, DCDA twins at 32 weeks, previous CS for failed induction. Mild abdominal discomfort. Soft, non-tender, overdistended uterus, normal FHRs. Irregular tightenings. Cervix: no dilatation, part above brim.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida induced for gestational diabetes at 38 weeks. After prolonged induction, fully dilated, vertex at +2 station, small caput, reassuring FHR. Has been pushing for 2 hours.

choices.label: A
choices.text: abandon vaginal delivery and proceed with Cat 1 LSCS

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Failed manual rotation and failed ventouse rotation — vertex at +1 station in persistent ROT position. After two failed attempts at rotation (manual and instrumental), this warrants category 1 LSCS under general anaesthesia (B). Kielland forceps could be considered by an experienced operator, but after failed rotation attempts, CS is safest.

choices.label: E
choices.text: epidural anaesthesia and continuous fetal heart monitoring

choices.label: G
choices.text: expectant management with continuous fetal heart monitoring
choices.explanation: Fully dilated, vertex at +2 station, reassuring FHR, small caput in a gestational diabetic — with good progress (vertex at +2) and reassuring CTG, expectant management with continuous CTG (G) and encouragement to push is appropriate. The fetal heart is reassuring, so immediate instrumental delivery is not required. She may deliver spontaneously with continued pushing.

choices.label: K
choices.text: manual rotation followed by forceps delivery

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: LOA position at pelvic floor level with late decelerations — pelvic floor level = outlet. With fetal compromise (late decelerations) and vertex at outlet in favourable position (LOA), outlet forceps (L) is appropriate. ITP with normal blood counts does not contraindicate instrumental delivery if platelet count is adequate.

choices.label: M
choices.text: rotation with vacuum cup followed by delivery

choices.label: N
choices.text: trial vaginal delivery in operating theatre

questions.stem: A 29-year-old with PPROM at 34 weeks, induced for raised CRP. Fully dilated, cervix fully dilated, vertex at +2 station, LOA position, late decelerations on CTG after 2 hours of pushing.

choices.label: L

LOA at +2 station = outlet delivery. Outlet forceps (L) or ventouse are appropriate. L (outlet forceps) is the best answer.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old second pregnancy, MCDA twins at 24 weeks: one twin's fetal demise, second twin well grown. Counselling offered, initial bloods sent.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Placental abruption with fetal demise and maternal haemodynamic instability (pallor, tachycardia, low BP) — this is a life-threatening obstetric emergency. With Bishop score 3 (unfavourable cervix), emergency LSCS is indicated (B) to deliver the placenta and control haemorrhage. Additionally, Kleihauer test and anti-D should be given for Rhesus negative mother.

choices.label: C
choices.text: commence intravenous antibiotics with anaerobic cover

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Intrauterine fetal death (IUFD) at 32 weeks in an otherwise healthy woman — counselling (D) is the immediate priority. SANDS guidelines emphasise that parents should be given time and support to process the loss, discuss options (expectant management, induction), and make decisions. Induction follows counselling.

choices.label: E
choices.text: expectant management and referral to higher centre
choices.explanation: Single fetal demise in MCDA (monochorionic diamniotic) twins — there is significant risk to the surviving twin due to shared placentation (haemodynamic instability, ischaemic injury). The management is expectant (E) — no immediate delivery needed unless the surviving twin is compromised. Referral to a higher centre/fetal medicine unit is essential for specialist management including MRI brain for the surviving twin.

choices.label: G
choices.text: HDU admission, correction of hypoglycaemia, coagulation and electrolytes

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: IUFD at 36 weeks, patient opts for immediate delivery. The standard management for IUFD is mifepristone (200 mg) 24-48 hours before vaginal misoprostol (J) — this combination is recommended in RCOG Green Top Guideline 55 for second and third trimester IUFD. It achieves successful vaginal delivery in >90% cases.

choices.label: L
choices.text: oxytocin infusion 5 units in 500 mL normal saline

choices.label: O
choices.text: vitamin K injection and fresh frozen plasma

SANDS guidelines emphasise that parents should be given time and support to process the loss, discuss options (expectant management, induction), and make decisions. Induction follows counselling.

choices.label: E
choices.text: expectant management and referral to higher centre

choices.label: G
choices.text: HDU admission, correction of hypoglycaemia, coagulation and electrolytes
choices.explanation: IUFD with severe liver disease (jaundice, deranged LFTs), coagulopathy (INR 3.0) and dehydration — this suggests acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP) or HELLP syndrome. Management priority is HDU admission, correction of coagulopathy (vitamin K and FFO), correction of electrolytes and glucose, before any obstetric intervention (G). The coagulopathy must be corrected before induction.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: IUFD at 36 weeks, patient opts for immediate delivery. The standard management for IUFD is mifepristone (200 mg) 24-48 hours before vaginal misoprostol (J) — this combination is recommended in RCOG Green Top Guideline 55 for second and third trimester IUFD. It achieves successful vaginal delivery in >90% cases.

choices.label: L
choices.text: oxytocin infusion 5 units in 500 mL normal saline

choices.label: O
choices.text: vitamin K injection and fresh frozen plasma

questions.topic: 1.20 Psychiatric Disease in Pregnancy and Puerperium
questions.optionsTitle: 1.20 Psychiatric Disease in Pregnancy and Puerperium
questions.stem: A 21-year-old with known bipolar disorder on regular medication, including fluoxetine, attends for preconception counselling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: advice on diet, exercise and folic acid
choices.explanation: Preconception counselling for a patient with bipolar disorder on fluoxetine should include: advice on diet, exercise and folic acid (C) as first steps, plus a medication review with the psychiatrist. Fluoxetine is relatively safe but should be reviewed. The patient needs information about the impact of pregnancy on bipolar disease and medication teratogenicity.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Grief reaction after perinatal bereavement — loss of a baby at term after a traumatic accident. The cabergoline has suppressed lactation (hence no engorgement). Being tearful is an expected grief reaction. Counselling and bereavement support (F) are the appropriate response.

choices.label: G
choices.text: counselling the partner and other family members

choices.label: H
choices.text: dedicated mother and baby unit admission
choices.explanation: Severe postnatal depression with suicidal ideation and self-harm thoughts, with dehydration and inability to care for herself — this requires admission to a dedicated mother and baby unit (H). This is a psychiatric emergency. She needs inpatient psychiatric care in a mother and baby unit where she can be treated while remaining with her baby.

choices.label: L
choices.text: involvement of special mental health team/psychiatrist
choices.explanation: Auditory hallucinations in a pregnant woman — this suggests an acute psychotic episode or relapse of a psychotic illness. Immediate involvement of the perinatal mental health team/psychiatrist (L) is required. This is a psychiatric emergency requiring specialist assessment.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old on 4th day after delivery: tired, lack of appetite, unwell, severe episiotomy pain, vaginal bleeding. Had prolonged labour, forceps delivery, very upset about the whole event. Normal physical examination.

choices.label: C
choices.text: advice on diet, exercise and folic acid

Fluoxetine is relatively safe but should be reviewed. The patient needs information about the impact of pregnancy on bipolar disease and medication teratogenicity.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: This presentation (day 4 postpartum, tearful, upset, normal physical findings) is consistent with 'baby blues' (postnatal emotional lability within the first week, typically days 3-5). Counselling (F) and reassurance are appropriate. Baby blues resolves spontaneously within a few days.

choices.label: G

questions.stem: A 22-year-old at 12 weeks: severe abdominal pain, severe nausea, no bleeding or urinary symptoms. The previous night she attended a party and consumed alcohol. BP 110/80, PR 98. Diffuse upper abdominal tenderness without rigidity. NT scan normal previous day.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Diffuse upper abdominal tenderness with nausea after alcohol consumption — this is acute severe gastritis (D) or alcoholic gastritis. Pancreatitis could also be considered, but the history of alcohol with diffuse rather than severe epigastric radiation, and no guarding, makes gastritis the most likely diagnosis.

choices.label: G
choices.text: incarceration of uterus with urinary retention
choices.explanation: Difficulty passing urine at 14 weeks with lower abdominal fullness in a multigravida — this is incarceration of the retroverted gravid uterus causing urinary retention (G). This occurs when the growing uterus becomes incarcerated in the pelvis. Catheterisation is the treatment.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Painful uterine fibroids in pregnancy, especially after steroid administration (which can initially worsen pain by causing fibroid swelling), with uterine tenderness but no placental or fetal compromise — this is red degeneration (carneous degeneration) of a fibroid (K). It occurs during rapid fibroid growth in pregnancy and causes severe localised pain.

choices.label: O
choices.explanation: Recurrent fever, flank/loin pain, dysuria and frequency in pregnancy with previous antibiotic course — this is urinary tract sepsis/recurrent pyelonephritis (O). The previous treatment may have been inadequate. Urgent MSU and IV antibiotics are required.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old third gravida at 14 weeks: abdominal discomfort and difficulty passing urine since afternoon. Normal NT scan last week. BP 120/80, PR 88. Fullness in lower abdomen, no rigidity or guarding.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Right-sided tenderness with guarding, rigidity, fever and leucocytosis — acute appendicitis (A). In pregnancy, the appendix is displaced upward and to the right. Right-sided peritonism with fever and leucocytosis strongly suggests appendicitis. Urgent surgical review and laparoscopy/laparotomy are needed.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Diffuse upper abdominal tenderness with nausea after alcohol consumption — this is acute severe gastritis (D) or alcoholic gastritis. questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 8 weeks with type 1 diabetes on insulin. BMI 28. Fasting glucose 6 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.0% (43 mmol/mol). Monitors glucose levels and has annual retinal screening.

choices.label: C
choices.text: fasting and postprandial sugar, HbA1c level monitoring

choices.label: D
choices.text: folic acid, diet and exercises along with monitoring
choices.explanation: Well-controlled type 1 diabetic in early pregnancy (HbA1c 6.0%, fasting glucose 6 mmol/L) who already monitors her glucose and has retinal screening. The next step in management includes continued dietary advice, exercise and folic acid supplementation (5 mg/day for type 1 diabetics due to NTD risk) with continued glucose monitoring (D).

choices.label: F
choices.text: induction of labour at 37 completed weeks

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Gestational diabetes with fasting glucose 7.8 mmol/L and HbA1c 8.0% — this is poorly controlled. A fasting glucose ≥7.0 or HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol indicates a need for insulin (H). Metformin alone would be insufficient for this level of hyperglycaemia. Insulin treatment (H) is indicated.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Type 2 diabetic with macrosomia (AC >97th centile), previous fetal death at 29 weeks, now at 37 completed weeks — induction of labour is offered (K). NICE NG3 guidelines recommend offering induction of labour to women with diabetes from 37+0 to 38+6 weeks. With macrosomia and previous IUFD, induction is strongly indicated.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Previous gestational diabetes with normal postpartum glucose — NICE recommends offering OGTT (75 g, 2-hour) at 24-28 weeks (and earlier if symptoms suggest) to women with previous GDM. At 13 weeks, if there are risk factors, an early OGTT can be done and if negative repeated at 24-28 weeks. L (OGTT) is the appropriate screening test.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks: BMI 28, polyhydramnios on growth scan. Normal 20-week anatomy scan. OGTT fasting 7.8 mmol/L, HbA1c 8.0% (64 mmol/mol). Normal renal parameters.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old at 36 weeks with previous cervical cerclage (placed at 14 weeks for 2 prior mid-trimester miscarriages). Contracting twice in 10 min, cervix 3 cm dilated, mild bleeding. Suture removed. Normal CTG. Normal vitals.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: At 39 weeks, regular contractions, 4 cm dilated with mild bloodstained discharge and reassuring CTG — this is active labour with a 'show'. The next step in active labour is amniotomy (A) to augment labour and establish a clear liquor colour to exclude fetal compromise.

choices.label: B
choices.text: betamethasone 12 mg two doses at 24 hours interval
choices.explanation: Painless vaginal bleeding at 30 weeks in a patient with a previous CS — placenta praevia must be excluded. However, the primary management of antepartum haemorrhage of unknown cause at 30 weeks includes betamethasone (B) for fetal lung maturity in case delivery becomes necessary, and ultrasound to exclude placenta praevia. B is the most appropriate immediate management.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Preterm labour at 36 weeks with cervical cerclage now removed — labour is progressing. With normal CTG and vitals, the management is continuous fetal heart monitoring (F) during labour. The cerclage has been appropriately removed. Labour will proceed and the baby at 36 weeks does not need betamethasone urgently (though one dose could be considered).

choices.label: I
choices.text: initial resuscitation followed by category I LSCS
choices.explanation: Placental abruption at 30 weeks with maternal haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) and fetal compromise (FHR 98 bpm, suspicious CTG) — this requires immediate resuscitation followed by Category I LSCS (I). This is a life-threatening emergency for both mother and baby.

choices.label: K
choices.text: IV access, CBC, coagulation profile, LFT, urea and electrolytes, cross-match 6 units

choices.label: L
choices.text: Kleihauer test and anti-D 300 mcg administration

choices.text: IV access, CBC, coagulation profile, LFT, urea and electrolytes, cross-match 6 units

choices.label: L
choices.text: Kleihauer test and anti-D 300 mcg administration

questions.stem: A 29-year-old second gravida at 30 weeks: small vaginal bleed, no abdominal pain. Haemodynamically stable. Soft non-tender uterus. Reassuring FHR. Previous LSCS at 34 weeks for SROM with breech.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: At 39 weeks, regular contractions, 4 cm dilated with mild bloodstained discharge and reassuring CTG — this is active labour with a 'show'. The next step in active labour is amniotomy (A) to augment labour and establish a clear liquor colour to exclude fetal compromise.

choices.label: B
choices.text: betamethasone 12 mg two doses at 24 hours interval
choices.explanation: Painless vaginal bleeding at 30 weeks in a patient with a previous CS — placenta praevia must be excluded. However, the primary management of antepartum haemorrhage of unknown cause at 30 weeks includes betamethasone (B) for fetal lung maturity in case delivery becomes necessary, and ultrasound to exclude placenta praevia. B is the most appropriate immediate management.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Preterm labour at 36 weeks with cervical cerclage now removed — labour is progressing. With normal CTG and vitals, the management is continuous fetal heart monitoring (F) during labour. The cerclage has been appropriately removed. Labour will proceed and the baby at 36 weeks does not need betamethasone urgently (though one dose could be considered).

choices.label: I
choices.text: initial resuscitation followed by category I LSCS
choices.explanation: Placental abruption at 30 weeks with maternal haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) and fetal compromise (FHR 98 bpm, suspicious CTG) — this requires immediate resuscitation followed by Category I LSCS (I). This is a life-threatening emergency for both mother and baby.

choices.label: K
choices.text: IV access, CBC, coagulation profile, LFT, urea and electrolytes, cross-match 6 units

choices.label: L
choices.text: Kleihauer test and anti-D 300 mcg administration

questions.stem: A 27-year-old primigravida at 20 weeks: severe breathlessness. Known hyperactive airway syndrome, stopped Montelukast when pregnancy confirmed. Afebrile, PR 100, BP 100/60, RR 40, SaO2 94%, PEFR 35%. Poor air entry both sides.

choices.label: C
choices.text: complete blood count, urea, creatinine, LFT and peripheral smear

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Right-sided pleuritic chest pain with tachycardia, breathlessness in a pregnant woman with risk factors (overweight, previous smoker) — suspected pulmonary embolism. PEFR >70% argues against asthma. CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) (E) is the investigation of choice for PE in pregnancy (preferred over V/Q scan in most UK centres). She is stable, so CTPA is safe.

choices.label: F
choices.text: high dependency care with high flow oxygen, antibiotics and hydration
choices.explanation: Community-acquired pneumonia in pregnancy with bilateral crepitations, purulent sputum, fever, dehydration and tachypnoea — this requires high dependency care with high-flow oxygen, IV antibiotics and hydration (F). Pneumonia in pregnancy can be severe and requires inpatient management.

choices.label: G
choices.text: immediate nebulisation, beta-agonist and steroid inhalers and start Montelukast
choices.explanation: Acute severe asthma in pregnancy (PEFR 35% — severe exacerbation criteria). Treatment: immediate nebulised salbutamol (beta-agonist), IV/nebulised steroids and restart Montelukast. Montelukast should NOT be stopped in pregnancy — stopping can precipitate asthma exacerbation. G (immediate nebulisation, beta-agonist, steroid inhalers, restart Montelukast) is correct.

choices.label: I
choices.text: inpatient care with hydration, antibiotics and physiotherapy

choices.label: K
choices.text: nutritional supplements with iron and folic acid
choices.explanation: Breathlessness in a pale, anaemic pregnant woman (Hb 9.0, MCV 65) — this is anaemia causing exertional dyspnoea. MCV 65 indicates microcytic anaemia, most likely iron deficiency. Management is nutritional supplements with iron and folic acid (K). The breathlessness is due to anaemia, not pulmonary disease.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at term +2 presented with reduced fetal movements and IUFD confirmed on USS. She had attended a few days ago with the same complaint, reassured after normal CTG. She and her partner are informed and counselled. She opts for induction the next day.

choices.label: A
choices.text: auditing the existing practice and outcome

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: A planned management for known placenta accreta that resulted in hysterectomy — this was managed correctly with appropriate counselling (C) and multidisciplinary planning. The counselling before the procedure and informed consent documentation are the key elements of good practice in this case.

choices.label: G
choices.text: immediate resuscitation and transfer to HDU
choices.explanation: Wrong blood transfusion reaction — this is immediately life-threatening. The transfusion must be stopped and G (immediate resuscitation and HDU transfer) is the immediate clinical response. This is also a serious RIDDOR/SHOT reportable incident, but the immediate priority is patient safety.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: An intrauterine fetal death following a recent presentation with reduced fetal movements, where the previous CTG was normal — this is a potentially serious incident that requires incident reporting (H). The case will then be reviewed to determine if the clinical management was appropriate and whether a serious untoward incident (SUI) investigation is needed.

choices.label: I
choices.text: initiate disciplinary action against the person involved

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: After the incident is reported, a root cause analysis (J) is required to understand how this severe genital synechia (likely FGM related) was not identified and managed antenatally, and to prevent similar cases. Root cause analysis identifies systemic failures and leads to service improvement.

choices.label: N
choices.text: revise the existing guidelines and disseminating to all antenatal staff

questions.stem: A 27-year-old second gravida referred for small dates at 28 weeks. BMI 18, negative first trimester screening. First pregnancy: 4.8 lb baby at term. USS: all fetal parameters <5th centile, normal Dopplers.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Symmetrical IUGR with echogenic bowel at 20 weeks — echogenic bowel is associated with cystic fibrosis, chromosomal abnormalities (particularly trisomy 21), CMV infection and IUGR. Amniocentesis (A) for karyotyping and CMV PCR should be offered, as the combination of early symmetrical IUGR and echogenic bowel is concerning for chromosomal or infectious cause.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: IUGR at 35 weeks with reversed end diastolic flow (REDF) in umbilical artery Doppler — this is severe fetal compromise and an indication for immediate delivery. Category 3 LSCS (G) is appropriate (or category 2 depending on fetal wellbeing). REDF is associated with high perinatal morbidity and mortality; immediate delivery is indicated at ≥34 weeks.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Low PAPP-A is a risk factor for fetal growth restriction, pre-eclampsia and preterm birth. The evidence-based intervention is low-dose aspirin (75-150 mg/day) from before 16 weeks to reduce the risk of pre-eclampsia and placental insufficiency (H). Serial growth scans are also recommended.

choices.label: J
choices.text: induction of labour with intravaginal prostaglandins

choices.label: M
choices.text: serial growth scans, fetal umbilical Doppler study
choices.explanation: SGA fetus at 28 weeks with normal Dopplers — this requires serial growth scans and fetal Doppler studies (M) every 2 weeks. Normal Doppler is reassuring but serial monitoring is essential to detect deterioration. Immediate delivery is not indicated with normal Dopplers.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old primigravida at 20 weeks: extreme lethargy and tiredness. Smokes 10 cigarettes/day, lives alone. Not seen since booking. Hb 9.0, MCV 70 fL, haematocrit 35%, serum ferritin 45 mcg/L, WBC 11×10⁹, platelets 375×10⁹.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Pancytopenia (anaemia Hb 5.0, leucopenia WBC 1×10⁹, thrombocytopenia 80×10⁹) with normal MCV — this is aplastic anaemia (C). Aplastic anaemia causes failure of all cell lines due to bone marrow failure. It can be triggered by viral infections. Bone marrow biopsy confirms the diagnosis.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Hypertension (160/110), significant proteinuria (3+), thrombocytopenia (platelets 50×10⁹), and elevated liver enzymes (implied) in a pregnant woman at 32 weeks — this is HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets). HELLP is a severe variant of pre-eclampsia. Immediate HDU care and delivery planning are required.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Chronic thrombocytopenia (platelets 80-100×10⁹) with longstanding history of menorrhagia and gum bleeding in a young woman without anaemia — this is idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) (I). ITP is an autoimmune condition causing platelet destruction, presenting with mucocutaneous bleeding and longstanding low platelet counts.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Anaemia (Hb 9.0) with low MCV (70) and low-normal ferritin (45) in a social isolate who smokes — this is iron deficiency anaemia (J). In pregnancy, ferritin <30 mcg/L is diagnostic of iron deficiency, but ferritin 45 can still represent relative deficiency given the increased demands. The microcytic picture with social risk factors points to nutritional iron deficiency.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old primigravida at 12 weeks: lethargic, generalised weakness. Hb 5.0, MCV 90, WBC 1×10⁹, platelets 80×10⁹. Pale, petechial spots (attributed to viral illness). Repeat bloods: similar results.

choices.explanation: Anaemia (Hb 9.0) with low MCV (70) and low-normal ferritin (45) in a social isolate who smokes — this is iron deficiency anaemia (J). In pregnancy, ferritin <30 mcg/L is diagnostic of iron deficiency, but ferritin 45 can still represent relative deficiency given the increased demands. The microcytic picture with social risk factors points to nutritional iron deficiency.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman: 6-month history of urinary urgency-frequency, no urge incontinence or nocturia. GP urinalysis done last fortnight was negative.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Urgency-frequency syndrome with negative urinalysis — before proceeding to urodynamics, a bladder diary (A) should be completed. The bladder diary documents frequency, volumes voided, urgency episodes, pad usage and fluid intake over 3 days. This provides objective evidence of the pattern and severity before further investigation or treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Chronic bladder pain with urgency, haematuria, and consistently negative cultures — this picture is highly suggestive of interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS). Cystoscopy and bladder biopsy (D) is the investigation of choice to confirm the diagnosis (shows Hunner ulcers or glomerulations) and exclude other pathology.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Haematuria (blood 3+ on dipstick) in a 72-year-old woman without urinary infection — this is an urgent red flag symptom for urothelial (bladder, renal pelvis) or renal malignancy. Referral under the 2-week rule (E) to haematuria clinic is mandatory in the UK for unexplained haematuria in patients over 45 years.

choices.label: K
choices.text: urine staining and culture for fungus

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Mixed urinary incontinence (stress + urgency) that has not responded to anticholinergics — urodynamics (L) is required before surgical intervention (e.g. TVT) to confirm the diagnosis, rule out detrusor overactivity or voiding dysfunction, and guide management. NICE guidelines recommend urodynamics before surgery for stress incontinence.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old: primary amenorrhoea, cyclical abdominal pain, increasing difficulty with (but not in) voiding urine and defecation.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cyclical pelvic pain with primary amenorrhoea and difficulty with voiding/defecation but not voiding itself — this is haematocolpos (A) due to imperforate hymen or transverse vaginal septum. Blood accumulates in the vagina causing a lower abdominal mass that compresses the bladder and rectum. The cyclical pain occurs with each menstrual cycle.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Persistent vaginal discharge and lower abdominal pain with long-term IUCD use — pelvic actinomycosis (G). Actinomyces-like organisms (ALOs) are associated with long-term IUCD use. The discharge from pelvic actinomycosis is typically not foul-smelling. Diagnosis is by cervical smear showing ALOs or culture.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Amenorrhoea after severe obstetric haemorrhage/difficult delivery with failure of lactation, weight gain, hypothyroidism, and loss of sense of smell (anosmia for coffee) — this is Sheehan's syndrome (H), pituitary necrosis following peripartum haemorrhage. It causes panhypopituitarism: no prolactin (no lactation), no TSH (hypothyroidism), no FSH/LH (amenorrhoea).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Secondary amenorrhoea from age 15-18 with wide carrying angle (cubitus valgus) — this is Turner's syndrome (J) with partial gonadal function (mosaic Turner's) that allowed initial menstruation but led to premature ovarian failure. Cubitus valgus is a classic feature of Turner's syndrome.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old: not having periods regularly. Menarche at 12, periods every 2-3 months. Normal BMI, plays sport regularly.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Post-coital and intermenstrual bleeding in a young sexually active woman on the COCP — the most important diagnosis to exclude is Chlamydia trachomatis cervicitis, which causes cervical inflammation and contact/intermenstrual bleeding. Chlamydia screening (B) is the first-line investigation.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Intermenstrual bleeding in a 40-year-old who has never had investigations — cervical smear (Pap smear) (I) should be the first investigation to exclude cervical neoplasia or infection. She may not have had UK cervical screening. Transvaginal USS and endometrial sampling would follow if smear is normal.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Irregular periods 1 year after menarche at age 12 — this is normal. The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis takes 2-3 years to mature after menarche, resulting in anovulatory cycles and irregular periods. With a normal BMI and otherwise healthy teenager, reassurance (J) is appropriate.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) — always requires investigation to exclude endometrial cancer. Transvaginal ultrasound (K) is the first-line investigation. Endometrial thickness <4 mm on TVUS makes endometrial cancer unlikely. If thickness >4 mm or unable to visualise, endometrial sampling is needed.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old: severe mood swings a week before her period, heavy bleeding during periods. Affects relationship with boyfriend and university performance.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Premenstrual mood symptoms of recent onset in a 43-year-old — assessment is needed first to determine if symptoms are truly premenstrual (CBT after diagnosis). Cognitive behavioural therapy (B) is recommended as first-line non-pharmacological treatment for PMS, particularly for psychological symptoms. A symptom diary is needed first to confirm the cyclical pattern.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PMS with mood symptoms and heavy menstrual bleeding in an 18-year-old requiring contraception as well — the combined oral contraceptive pill with drospirenone (E) (e.g. Yasmin) is ideal. Drospirenone has anti-aldosterone and anti-androgenic properties, which help with bloating, mood and skin. It also reduces heavy menstrual bleeding.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Predominantly physical PMS symptoms (bloating, fluid retention) without mood symptoms — spironolactone (J), an aldosterone antagonist/diuretic, is effective for premenstrual fluid retention and bloating. It is used in the luteal phase.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Cyclical mood symptoms (low mood) that correlate with the menstrual cycle suggest PMDD (premenstrual dysphoric disorder). When counselling is refused, first-line pharmacological treatment is an SSRI (K) (e.g. fluoxetine, sertraline). SSRIs can be taken continuously or in the luteal phase only.

choices.label: M
choices.text: total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy

questions.stem: A 30-year-old couple: trying to conceive for 2 years. Regular cycles, normal BMI. Semenalysis: 2 mL, count 4 million/mL, 5% normal forms, 35% progressive motility.

choices.label: A
choices.text: antral follicle count on transvaginal ultrasound

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HSG showing bilateral tubal occlusion at the cornua — this suggests proximal tubal obstruction, which may be spasm during the procedure (common) or true occlusion. Laparoscopy with dye test (D) is needed to confirm tubal patency and assess for pelvic endometriosis or adhesions. Laparoscopy can also treat reversible causes.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: The semenalysis shows severe oligoasthenoteratozoospermia (count <15 million/mL, <4% normal morphology, <32% progressive motility). This result needs REPEAT semenalysis (F) to confirm the abnormal result, as a single abnormal semenalysis should be repeated after 3 months. If confirmed, male factor subfertility is the primary issue requiring urological review.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Amenorrhoea with headache and bitemporal visual field defect (difficulty looking out of corners of both eyes) — this is a classic presentation of a pituitary macroadenoma (prolactinoma). Serum prolactin (K) is the first-line investigation. Bitemporal hemianopia results from compression of the optic chiasm by the tumour.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old: secondary subfertility, 7-year-old child, regular cycles until a year ago. Now very slight bleeding, no period for 3 months. Vague headache, difficulty looking from the corner of both eyes. Negative UPT.

choices.label: A
choices.text: antral follicle count on transvaginal ultrasound

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HSG showing bilateral tubal occlusion at the cornua — this suggests proximal tubal obstruction, which may be spasm during the procedure (common) or true occlusion. Laparoscopy with dye test (D) is needed to confirm tubal patency and assess for pelvic endometriosis or adhesions. Laparoscopy can also treat reversible causes.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: HIV-positive man wishing to father a child — with an undetectable viral load (<200 copies/mL), the risk of HIV transmission through natural conception is extremely low (U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable). The female partner's fertility should be assessed, starting with reviewing semenalysis from the male partner (F) to evaluate sperm parameters before referring for assisted conception.

choices.explanation: Amenorrhoea with headache and bitemporal visual field defect (difficulty looking out of corners of both eyes) — this is a classic presentation of a pituitary macroadenoma (prolactinoma). Serum prolactin (K) is the first-line investigation. Bitemporal hemianopia results from compression of the optic chiasm by the tumour.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old: 6-week amenorrhoea, severe lower abdominal pain and tenesmus. Positive UPT. USS: left adnexal sac, free fluid in pelvis, empty uterine cavity. Poor tolerance of scan due to tenderness. Beta-hCG 5400 IU. Haemodynamically stable.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Small adnexal mass (8×4 mm), no free fluid, very low beta-hCG (150 IU) — this meets criteria for expectant management (B) of a suspected ectopic pregnancy. Serial beta-hCG monitoring every 48 hours is required. If beta-hCG falls, expectant management is continued. NICE guidelines support expectant management for small, asymptomatic ectopics.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Very low gestational sac with no sliding sign (= sac does not move with probe pressure) and blood flow seen below the internal os — this is a cervical ectopic pregnancy or Caesarean scar ectopic. Rising beta-hCG (900→1118) confirms viability. Intramuscular methotrexate (C) is first-line treatment for cervical ectopic/scar ectopic with rising beta-hCG.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Ectopic pregnancy (beta-hCG 5400, adnexal sac, free fluid) in a haemodynamically stable 25-year-old with 6 weeks amenorrhoea — surgical management indicated (beta-hCG >1500 with adnexal mass). Laparoscopic approach is preferred. In a young woman wanting to preserve fertility, laparoscopic salpingotomy (F) is the preferred approach. Salpingectomy would be preferred if contralateral tube is damaged or bleeding is severe.

choices.label: K
choices.text: ultrasound guided methotrexate into the sac

flag.source: RCOG GTG 21; NICE NG126; ESEP trial (Lancet 2014)
flag.note: With a HEALTHY contralateral tube, laparoscopic salpingectomy is the recommended first-line surgical treatment — salpingotomy gives no proven fertility benefit and carries higher rates of persistent trophoblast and repeat ectopic. Salpingotomy is reserved for women wishing to preserve fertility when the other tube is damaged/absent (not stated here).

questions.stem: A 24-year-old at 6 weeks: moderate vaginal bleeding, crampy abdominal pain. Positive UPT 10 days ago. Speculum: partially open cervix. USS: open internal os, mixed echoes in cervical canal, small gestational sac with yolk sac, sliding sign positive.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Open cervix with products in the cervical canal but a gestational sac still in the uterine cavity — this is an inevitable/incomplete miscarriage. The sliding sign positive suggests the sac is not implanted in the cervix. With an open cervix, expectant management (A) is reasonable as the miscarriage is already progressing. Medical or surgical management can also be offered.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Missed miscarriage (silent miscarriage) — management options include expectant, medical or surgical. Medical management with misoprostol is a common choice. The recommended NICE dose for medical management of missed miscarriage is intravaginal misoprostol 800 mcg (D). However, for early missed miscarriage (<9+0 weeks), 600 mcg PV is also used (C). The textbook answer based on UK EPU practice is D (800 mcg) or C.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Histology showing decidua but no chorionic villi after evacuation for missed miscarriage — this means there was no intrauterine pregnancy and the diagnosis may be an ectopic pregnancy that was missed, or a very early complete spontaneous miscarriage that passed before the evacuation. Serum beta-hCG (J) must be checked urgently to exclude ongoing ectopic pregnancy.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Following incomplete miscarriage that appears to be resolving (bleeding stopping, os closing) — confirmation of complete miscarriage can be done with urine beta-hCG after 3 weeks (K). If negative, it confirms complete miscarriage. TVUS could also be done but a simple urine hCG is sufficient and recommended in this scenario.

flag.note: For a MISSED miscarriage, NICE NG126 recommends a single dose of misoprostol 800 micrograms (vaginal/oral/sublingual). 600 micrograms is the dose for INCOMPLETE miscarriage — the file has the two doses swapped. (NICE 2023 notes 800 mcg may also be used for incomplete miscarriage to align protocols, so 800 mcg is the safe answer here.)

questions.stem: A 26-year-old: USS for menorrhagia shows anechoic ovarian cyst 28×37 mm right side. No other abnormal findings.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: A small, simple (anechoic, thin-walled) ovarian cyst ≤50 mm in a premenopausal woman — expectant management (D) is appropriate. RCOG/BSGE guidelines state that simple unilocular cysts ≤50 mm in premenopausal women can be managed conservatively with a follow-up USS in 4-8 weeks to confirm resolution.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A postmenopausal woman with a growing simple ovarian cyst (46→58 mm over 2 months) despite normal CA125 — RCOG guidelines suggest surgical management for postmenopausal cysts that grow or are >50 mm. Laparoscopic removal of cyst (G) is appropriate for a 58 mm simple cyst with normal CA125 in a fit postmenopausal woman.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old: abdominal pain, USS shows 46 mm thin-walled cyst, normal CA125. 2-month follow-up: 58 mm cyst, similar features, normal tumour markers. No new symptoms.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Small, simple, stable ovarian cyst (22-28 mm) in a postmenopausal woman with normal CA125 and RMI 100 (low risk) — the cyst has not grown and has normal markers. RCOG guidelines suggest annual USS for up to 3 years for simple cysts <50 mm with normal CA125 in postmenopausal women. Expectant management (D) continues.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: A postmenopausal woman with a growing simple ovarian cyst (46→58 mm over 2 months) despite normal CA125 — RCOG guidelines suggest surgical management for postmenopausal cysts that grow or are >50 mm. Laparoscopic removal of cyst (G) is appropriate for a 58 mm simple cyst with normal CA125 in a fit postmenopausal woman.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old: non-specific abdominal pain, ovarian cyst 28 mm, fluid filled. Ca125 normal, RMI 100. Follow-up scan 4 months later: unchanged, 22 mm.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Small, simple, stable ovarian cyst (22-28 mm) in a postmenopausal woman with normal CA125 and RMI 100 (low risk) — the cyst has not grown and has normal markers. questions.stem: A 23-year-old: bilateral lower abdominal pain, diffuse tenderness, febrile, period was 3 weeks ago. Negative UPT. Congested cervix and cervical excitation on speculum.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Fever, nausea/vomiting >24 hours, abdominal pain with cervical excitation but negative UPT — the prolonged nausea/vomiting with pyrexia and peritoneal signs suggests acute appendicitis (A). Cervical excitation can occur with any peritoneal irritation. The prolonged course with fever points to appendicitis rather than PID which typically has a shorter course.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sudden severe abdominal pain with fainting (haemodynamic compromise) in a woman with irregular periods using a non-compliant POP (at risk of pregnancy) — this is a ruptured ectopic pregnancy (B) until proven otherwise. Emergency evaluation with USS and beta-hCG is required immediately.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Pain at mid-cycle (2 weeks after last period) in a regular-cycle woman, negative pregnancy test, afebrile, resolves spontaneously with analgesia — this is Mittelschmerz (H), mid-cycle ovulation pain. It results from follicle rupture and release of follicular fluid causing peritoneal irritation. It is benign and self-limiting.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Bilateral pelvic pain, fever, cervical excitation (chandelier sign), negative pregnancy test — this is pelvic inflammatory disease (K). PID is caused by ascending infection from the cervix/vagina. Cervical excitation is pathognomonic of PID. Treatment is with appropriate antibiotics per BASHH guidelines.
