questions.stem: A 52-year-old: low libido. Attained menopause 5 years ago, taking estrogen and progesterone HRT.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Osteopenia with strong family history of breast cancer — HRT is relatively contraindicated (family history of breast cancer). Alendronate (A), a bisphosphonate, is indicated for osteopenia/osteoporosis when HRT is contraindicated. It reduces fracture risk. Tibolone and HRT would be inappropriate given the breast cancer family history.

choices.label: E
choices.text: estrogen and progesterone continuous combined HRT

choices.label: F
choices.text: estrogen and progesterone cyclical oral hormone replacement

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Severe menopausal symptoms (hot flushes) in a woman with a hysterectomy — no uterus means no need for progesterone, so estrogen-only oral HRT (G) is appropriate. Hypertension, if well-controlled, is not a contraindication to HRT.

choices.label: M
choices.explanation: Low libido in a menopausal woman already on combined HRT — the HRT has not addressed low libido. Testosterone therapy (M) is evidence-based for hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) in postmenopausal women. Testosterone replacement (gel or patch) significantly improves libido and sexual function in this setting.

choices.label: P
choices.explanation: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM) — dysuria, vaginal irritation, urethral caruncle (a benign urethral outgrowth related to oestrogen deficiency). Vaginal estrogen tablet (P) (e.g. Vagifem) is the treatment of choice for local GSM symptoms. Local estrogen is safe and effective, with minimal systemic absorption.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old: menarche one year ago, only two cycles in the year. Weighs 48 kg, normal BMI. Mother has PCOS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: combined oral contraceptive pill with cyproterone

choices.label: C
choices.text: combined oral contraceptive pill with drospirenone

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PCOS with primary complaint of inability to lose weight, normal menstrual cycles — orlistat (E) is a lipase inhibitor for obesity management. It is appropriate for PCOS patients whose primary issue is weight management when lifestyle modifications have failed.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: PCOS patient wanting to conceive with irregular cycles and normal BMI — weight loss is not applicable (normal BMI). Clomifene citrate (clomiphene) is first-line for ovulation induction. If clomifene fails, laparoscopic ovarian drilling (F) is an option for PCOS-related anovulatory infertility.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Irregular cycles in the first year after menarche is a normal finding due to HPO axis immaturity. With a normal BMI and otherwise healthy teenager, no treatment is needed — reassurance (H) is appropriate.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: PCOS with amenorrhoea of 6 months — even without oestrogen deficiency symptoms, long periods of anovulation/amenorrhoea in PCOS can lead to endometrial hyperplasia. Regular progestogen withdrawal bleeding (I) (e.g. medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg for 10-14 days every 3-4 months) is recommended to prevent endometrial hyperplasia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: combined oral contraceptive pill with cyproterone

choices.label: C
choices.text: combined oral contraceptive pill with drospirenone

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PCOS with primary complaint of inability to lose weight, normal menstrual cycles — orlistat (E) is a lipase inhibitor for obesity management. It is appropriate for PCOS patients whose primary issue is weight management when lifestyle modifications have failed.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: PCOS patient wanting to conceive with irregular cycles and normal BMI — weight loss is not applicable (normal BMI). Clomifene citrate (clomiphene) is first-line for ovulation induction. If clomifene fails, laparoscopic ovarian drilling (F) is an option for PCOS-related anovulatory infertility.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Irregular cycles in the first year after menarche is a normal finding due to HPO axis immaturity. With a normal BMI and otherwise healthy teenager, no treatment is needed — reassurance (H) is appropriate.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: PCOS with amenorrhoea of 6 months — even without oestrogen deficiency symptoms, long periods of anovulation/amenorrhoea in PCOS can lead to endometrial hyperplasia. Regular progestogen withdrawal bleeding (I) (e.g. medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg for 10-14 days every 3-4 months) is recommended to prevent endometrial hyperplasia.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old recently relocated to England: abdominal pain, 6-month-old child delivered by CS. Type 3 FGM found on examination.

choices.label: B
choices.text: counsel about illegal nature of act
choices.explanation: Reinfibulation (re-stitching after delivery to restore the infibulated state) is illegal under the Female Genital Mutilation Act 2003 in the UK. The patient must be counselled about the illegal nature of reinfibulation (B). Healthcare professionals cannot perform or facilitate reinfibulation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: elective division of scar tissue before onset of labour

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Type 3 FGM (infibulation) found in a 17-year-old (minor) in England — FGM in a person under 18 is a child protection concern. The health professional must inform the police (F) about FGM identified in a minor. This is mandatory under the Female Genital Mutilation Act 2003 (as amended 2015) — mandatory reporting to police for FGM in under-18s.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Type 2 FGM with visible urethra at 25 weeks — type 2 involves partial or total removal of the clitoris and/or labia minora. If the urethra is visible and accessible, catheterisation in labour should be possible. Deinfibulation may not be necessary if the urethral and vaginal opening are adequate for delivery. Reassurance (I) and assessment in labour (A) with senior input is appropriate.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: A 12-year-old at risk of FGM — the mother has explicitly stated intent to take the girl abroad for FGM. This is a safeguarding emergency. Referral to social services (K) is required immediately to protect the child from FGM, which is illegal in the UK even if performed abroad (FGM Act 2003).

questions.stem: A 36-year-old: routine cervical screening, no gynaecological problems. IUCD in situ. Report: negative for CIN, actinomyces-like organisms (ALOs) seen.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Endometrial cell clusters on cervical smear in a 45-year-old woman with LMP 12 days ago (mid-cycle, no menstruation expected) — finding endometrial cells outside the menstrual phase in a woman ≥40 years requires endometrial sampling (A) to exclude endometrial pathology (hyperplasia or carcinoma).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Asymptomatic ALOs on cervical smear with IUCD in situ — if there are no symptoms of pelvic actinomycosis (no pelvic pain, discharge, or pyrexia), reassurance (C) is appropriate. The IUCD does not need to be removed. If the patient develops symptoms of PID, the IUCD should be removed.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Clue cells on cervical smear = bacterial vaginosis (BV). BV is caused by Gardnerella vaginalis and associated with clue cells (epithelial cells covered with bacteria). Treatment is metronidazole orally (J) (400 mg twice daily for 5-7 days) — standard first-line treatment for BV.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old: routine smear, no gynaecological symptoms. Report: negative for CIN, Candida spores noted.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Endometrial cell clusters on cervical smear in a 45-year-old woman with LMP 12 days ago (mid-cycle, no menstruation expected) — finding endometrial cells outside the menstrual phase in a woman ≥40 years requires endometrial sampling (A) to exclude endometrial pathology (hyperplasia or carcinoma).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Incidental finding of Candida on cervical smear in an asymptomatic woman — no treatment is needed. Candida is a commensal organism and treating asymptomatic women is not recommended. Reassurance (C) is appropriate.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Clue cells on cervical smear = bacterial vaginosis (BV). BV is caused by Gardnerella vaginalis and associated with clue cells (epithelial cells covered with bacteria). Treatment is metronidazole orally (J) (400 mg twice daily for 5-7 days) — standard first-line treatment for BV.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old: first routine cervical screening. Report: borderline squamous cell changes.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Low-grade dyskaryosis with negative HR-HPV test of triage — routine recall (E) for 3-yearly screening. Negative HR-HPV in the context of low-grade cytological changes is very reassuring and return to routine screening is appropriate.

choices.label: G
choices.text: repeat smear with GP after 6 months

choices.label: H
choices.text: routine referral to colposcopy in 6 weeks
choices.explanation: Borderline cytology with positive HR-HPV — routine referral to colposcopy in 6 weeks (H) as per NHSCSP guidelines. This is not an emergency; routine colposcopy within 6 weeks is appropriate.

choices.label: I
choices.text: see and treat colposcopy in 2 weeks
choices.explanation: High-grade moderate dyskaryosis — colposcopy and treatment (see and treat) in 2 weeks (I). High-grade changes (moderate or severe dyskaryosis/CIN2/3) require prompt colposcopy with LLETZ at the same visit (see and treat) or biopsy-guided treatment.

choices.label: J
choices.text: test of cure — High-risk HPV DNA

choices.label: K
choices.text: test of triage — High-risk HPV DNA test
choices.explanation: Borderline squamous changes on cervical cytology — the management is a test of triage with high-risk HPV DNA (K) (reflexive HPV testing). If HR-HPV is negative, routine recall. If HR-HPV positive, refer to colposcopy. This is the UK NHSCSP algorithm for borderline changes.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old: repeat smear done as previous routine smear 6 months ago was inadequate. Second sample now also reported as inadequate.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Two consecutive inadequate cervical smears → refer to colposcopy (A: two-week referral to colposcopy). NHSCSP 2016 guidance and BSCCP guidelines are unambiguous: if a second smear is also inadequate, the woman must be referred to colposcopy rather than repeating in the community a third time. Colposcopy allows direct visualisation and assessment of the transformation zone.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Endometrial cell clusters on smear in a 43-year-old woman with LMP 10 days ago — finding endometrial cells outside of menstruation (LMP was 10 days ago, smear done after 10 days means post-menstrual phase) in a woman ≥40 years requires investigation. Two-week referral to gynaecology clinic (B) for endometrial assessment to exclude endometrial pathology.

choices.label: F
choices.text: repeat smear with GP after 6 months

choices.label: H
choices.text: routine referral to colposcopy in 6 weeks

choices.label: I
choices.text: see and treat colposcopy in 2 weeks
choices.explanation: CGIN (cervical glandular intraepithelial neoplasia) on cervical cytology — this is a glandular abnormality and requires prompt colposcopy. See and treat colposcopy in 2 weeks (I) is appropriate. CGIN requires LLETZ (excision of the transformation zone) as biopsy alone may miss skip lesions.

choices.text: test of cure — High-risk HPV DNA

choices.label: K
choices.text: test of triage — High-risk HPV DNA test
choices.explanation: Borderline dyskaryosis at cervical screening — test of triage with HR-HPV DNA (K). If HR-HPV negative, routine recall. If positive, referral to colposcopy. This is the NHSCSP pathway.

questions.topic: 3.16 Risk of Complications in Abdominal Hysterectomy
questions.optionsTitle: 3.16 Risk of Complications in Abdominal Hysterectomy
questions.stem: The overall risk of the patient having any complication after abdominal hysterectomy for benign reasons.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The overall complication rate for abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 4 in 100 (4%). This includes all major and minor complications — infection, haemorrhage, urinary tract injury, thromboembolism etc.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: The risk of thromboembolic complications (DVT or PE) after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 4 in 1000 (0.4%). This is reduced with appropriate thromboprophylaxis (LMWH, TED stockings, early mobilisation).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: The risk of return to theatre after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 7 in 1000 (0.7%), for reasons including haematoma, haemorrhage or wound dehiscence.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: The risk of requiring blood transfusion during or after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 23 in 1000 (2.3%). This is a significant but recognised complication that should be discussed in preoperative counselling.

questions.topic: 3.16 Risk of Complications in Abdominal Hysterectomy
questions.optionsTitle: 3.16 Risk of Complications in Abdominal Hysterectomy
questions.stem: The risk of damage to the urinary system, including the ureter and the bladder, and long-term urinary dysfunction.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The overall complication rate for abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 4 in 100 (4%). This includes all major and minor complications — infection, haemorrhage, urinary tract injury, thromboembolism etc.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: The risk of thromboembolic complications (DVT or PE) after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 4 in 1000 (0.4%). This is reduced with appropriate thromboprophylaxis (LMWH, TED stockings, early mobilisation).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: The risk of urinary tract injury (ureter or bladder) during abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 7 in 1000 (0.7%). Long-term urinary dysfunction (incontinence, voiding problems) is also included in this estimate.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: The risk of requiring blood transfusion during or after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 23 in 1000 (2.3%). This is a significant but recognised complication that should be discussed in preoperative counselling.

questions.topic: 3.16 Risk of Complications in Abdominal Hysterectomy
questions.optionsTitle: 3.16 Risk of Complications in Abdominal Hysterectomy
questions.stem: Loss of blood requiring a blood transfusion.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The overall complication rate for abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 4 in 100 (4%). This includes all major and minor complications — infection, haemorrhage, urinary tract injury, thromboembolism etc.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: The risk of thromboembolic complications (DVT or PE) after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 4 in 1000 (0.4%). This is reduced with appropriate thromboprophylaxis (LMWH, TED stockings, early mobilisation).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: The risk of return to theatre after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 7 in 1000 (0.7%), for reasons including haematoma, haemorrhage or wound dehiscence.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: The risk of requiring blood transfusion during or after abdominal hysterectomy is approximately 23 in 1000 (2.3%). This is a significant but recognised complication that should be discussed in preoperative counselling.

questions.topic: 3.17 Risks Following Repair of Obstetric Anal Sphincter Injury
questions.optionsTitle: 3.17 Risks Following Repair of Obstetric Anal Sphincter Injury
questions.stem: An overwhelming desire to pass stools (faecal urgency) after repair of obstetric anal sphincter injury.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Wound sepsis/infection at the sphincter repair site occurs in approximately 8 in 100 women (8%). Appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis and postoperative wound care reduce this risk.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Perineal pain and dyspareunia after OASI repair occurs in approximately 9 in 100 women (9%). This is important to discuss as it affects quality of life and sexual relationships postoperatively.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Faecal urgency following OASI repair affects approximately 26 in 100 women (26%). Despite surgical repair, sphincter function may remain compromised, leading to urgency and inability to delay defaecation. This should be discussed in preoperative counselling.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old, two normal deliveries, last birth 6 months ago, regular periods, requesting copper IUCD. Wishes to know failure rate.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: The subdermal contraceptive implant (Nexplanon) has the lowest failure rate of all reversible contraception — approximately 1 per 1000 (0.1%) or 99.9% effective. It is more effective than sterilisation and is fully reversible upon removal.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: The injectable contraceptive (Depo-Provera/DMPA) has a failure rate of approximately 4 per 1000 (0.4%) with perfect use. With typical use, effectiveness is approximately 94-99%. It is one of the most effective reversible contraceptive methods.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: The levonorgestrel-releasing IUS (Mirena) has a failure rate of approximately 10 per 1000 (1%) over 5 years. It is also highly effective and additionally treats heavy menstrual bleeding.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: The copper IUCD (IUD) has a failure rate of approximately 20 per 1000 women over 5 years (Pearl index ~0.1-0.8/100 woman-years). However, in terms of per-insertion failure rate quoted in counselling, it is approximately 20 per 1000 (2%). The copper IUCD is highly effective long-acting contraception.

questions.stem: Menstruation has not started over the age of 16, with normal secondary sexual characteristics.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Asherman's syndrome: intrauterine adhesions following curettage → amenorrhoea or hypomenorrhoea.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Pituitary cause: prolactinoma → hyperprolactinaemia → inhibits GnRH → secondary amenorrhoea + galactorrhoea.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Primary amenorrhoea: absence of menarche by age 16 with normal secondary sexual characteristics (or by 14 with no pubertal development).

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Secondary amenorrhoea: cessation of menses for >6 months in a woman who previously menstruated (excluding pregnancy/menopause).

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with an 18-month history of secondary amenorrhoea.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Pituitary cause: prolactinoma → hyperprolactinaemia → inhibits GnRH → secondary amenorrhoea + galactorrhoea.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Physiological amenorrhoea: menopause. Average age 51-52, 12 months of amenorrhoea defines menopause.

choices.label: K

choices.label: L

questions.stem: A new mother 6 weeks postpartum who is breast feeding who has not started to menstruate again.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Asherman's syndrome: intrauterine adhesions following curettage → amenorrhoea or hypomenorrhoea.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Hypothalamic amenorrhoea (functional): low BMI, excessive exercise → reduced GnRH pulsatility → low FSH/LH.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Turner syndrome (45,XO): ovarian dysgenesis → hypergonadotrophic hypogonadism → requires hormone replacement.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Physiological amenorrhoea: lactation-induced. Prolactin suppresses GnRH → anovulation.

questions.stem: Empty uterus, free fluid within the pouch of Douglas. Serum bHCG is >1000 IU/mL.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ectopic pregnancy: empty uterus + free fluid (haemoperitoneum) + bHCG >1000 IU/mL with no IUP on TVS.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Missed miscarriage: embryo present but no heartbeat at 6+ weeks (or CRL >7mm without heartbeat).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Pregnancy of uncertain viability: at 5 weeks heartbeat may not yet be visible → repeat scan in 7-14 days.

questions.stem: A premenopausal patient with almost certain malignancy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Laparoscopic oophorectomy (conservative removal of affected ovary, preserve fertility).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Primary chemotherapy (poor surgical candidate due to COPD).

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Secondary chemotherapy (dysgerminoma is chemosensitive; BEP regimen).

choices.label: H
choices.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy, BSO and omentectomy through midline incision
choices.explanation: Midline (vertical) incision is mandatory for staging laparotomy in suspected ovarian malignancy — allows para-aortic node sampling, peritoneal washings, omentectomy, and adequate exposure. Low transverse (Pfannenstiel) incision is contraindicated. (Impey's O&G 5th ed.; RCOG GTG 2016)

choices.label: I
choices.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy, BSO and omentectomy through low transverse incision

questions.stem: A 35 year old, P3, undergoing laparoscopy for an ovarian cyst. At surgery, the cyst is found to be complex, measuring >5cm. Its characteristics are not consistent with a dermoid cyst or endometrioma; there is ascites.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Laparoscopic oophorectomy (conservative removal of affected ovary, preserve fertility).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Primary chemotherapy (poor surgical candidate due to COPD).

choices.label: H
choices.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy, BSO and omentectomy through midline incision

choices.label: I
choices.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy, BSO and omentectomy through low transverse incision

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Tumour debulking (J) / cytoreductive surgery to relieve bowel obstruction in advanced ovarian cancer. The goal is optimal debulking (residual disease <1 cm). She has no contraindications (no COPD, no comorbidities). (ESGO/BGCS guidelines; Impey's O&G 5th ed.)

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Suspicious ovarian cyst with ascites at laparoscopy → proceed to full laparotomy (K) for formal staging. Do NOT attempt laparoscopic excision of a potentially malignant cyst (risk of rupture and upstaging). Radiotherapy is not a surgical decision made intraoperatively. (RCOG GTG 2003; BGCS guidelines)

questions.stem: A 25-year-old girl presents with a 5-year history of daily pelvic pain; she is not currently in a relationship. She finds intercourse very painful with both superficial and deep dyspareunia and believes her pain to be the reason for the break-up of her relationship 2/12 ago. She has previously been investigated for this pain and has had three negative laparoscopies. She has also been seen by the gastroenterologist and has been given a diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Chronic PID: recurrent infections, infertility, deep dyspareunia, history of treated STIs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Chronic pelvic pain without organic pathology; negative laparoscopies suggest central sensitisation/functional pain.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Irritable bowel syndrome: pain relieved by defecation, associated with change in bowel habit.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Pelvic congestion syndrome: dull ache, worse premenstrually, worse with standing, relieved by lying down.

questions.stem: A woman who has had a total abdominal hysterectomy for uterine fibroids aged 40 and 3 years later has menopausal symptoms.

choices.label: F

questions.stem: A woman at 32/40 is reviewed in the day assessment unit. She has a blood pressure of 150/90mmHg and 3+ proteinuria.

choices.explanation: Maternal and fetal compromise → delivery by caesarean section.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Gestational hypertension at term → induction of labour (NICE guidance).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Severe hypertension: oral labetalol (or nifedipine) to control BP, aim for <160/100.

questions.stem: Hb 9.5 g/dL. The patient is asymptomatic. Blood film shows reduced MCV and MCH. Ferritin levels are reduced.

choices.explanation: Sickle cell crisis: dehydration/hyperemesis precipitates sickling → haemolytic anaemia + functional asplenia.

questions.stem: A woman, para 1, with a previous caesarean, is augmented in labour, at term, with Syntocinon. She has had a pinkish loss since her membranes were ruptured, but her loss becomes more brisk, her urine is bloody and for the last 30 minutes she has complained of pain between contractions across her lower abdomen. The CTG is abnormal.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Placental abruption: concealed haemorrhage, woody/tender uterus, maternal collapse, risk factors (smoking).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Placenta praevia: painless recurrent APH, fresh blood, history of low placenta.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Uterine rupture: previous CS, oxytocin augmentation, acute abdominal pain, haematuria, fetal distress.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Vasa praevia: fetal vessels over membranes; rupture causes massive fetal haemorrhage → bradycardia.

questions.stem: A multiparous woman, who has had one previous Ventouse delivery, is on all fours on the floor in the second stage of labour. The fetal head is at +2 station, direct OA position.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Spinal anaesthesia (B) is the standard for trial of rotational instrumental delivery (Kielland's forceps) in theatre. Spinal provides reliable dense block rapidly. GA is reserved for failed spinal or category-1 emergency CS — this stem is a trial of instrumental, not an emergency CS. (RCOG GTG 26 2011; Impey's O&G 5th ed.)

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Epidural recommended for multiple pregnancy to facilitate manipulation for second twin.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: General anaesthesia for emergency CS due to cord prolapse (urgency, need rapid uterine relaxation).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Imminent delivery → supportive care only (no time for other analgesia).

questions.stem: A woman at 39 weeks, who has had a previous caesarean section for breech presentation, wishes to try a vaginal delivery in this pregnancy.

choices.label: A

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Unengaged head at term + postdates → induction of labour (exclude cephalopelvic disproportion).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: VBAC: await spontaneous labour (caesarean section not indicated without other risk factors).

questions.stem: An Afro-Caribbean woman presents with chest pain and fever. Her GP has been treating her for hyperemesis and she appears dehydrated. Her Hb is 7.5 g/dL, and she has a high reticulocyte count. Blood film shows target cells and Howell-Jolly bodies.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Sickle cell crisis: dehydration triggers sickling → haemolytic anaemia (reticulocytosis), target cells, Howell-Jolly bodies (functional asplenia).

flag.appAnswer: C — Commence 10 mg oral vitamin K
flag.correctAnswer: G — Ursodeoxycholic acid (vitamin K only if abnormal PT)
flag.source: RCOG Green-top — Intrahepatic Cholestasis of Pregnancy (2022)
flag.note: For obstetric cholestasis, vitamin K is recommended ONLY when there is reduced fat absorption or a prolonged prothrombin time (neither stated here). The symptomatic drug treatment is ursodeoxycholic acid (option G) — note RCOG 2022 advises UDCA does not improve perinatal outcomes but it remains used for pruritus. The sibling cholestasis item in this pack is correctly keyed to UDCA.

flag.source: NICE NG123; UK mesh high-vigilance restriction (2018, Cumberlege review)
flag.note: Mid-urethral mesh tape is the internationally recognised gold-standard surgical treatment for stress incontinence and is a correct answer here. Currency note: in the UK, mid-urethral mesh slings have been under a high-vigilance restriction/pause since 2018, so colposuspension or an autologous fascial sling are the non-mesh alternatives offered (NICE NG123). Worth knowing for UK-style exams.

questions.topic: Maternal and perinatal mortality: the confidential enquiry
questions.optionsTitle: Maternal and perinatal mortality: the confidential enquiry
questions.stem: Death of a woman while pregnant, or within 42 days of termination of pregnancy, from any cause related to, or aggravated by, the pregnancy or its management, but not from accidental or incidental death.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Maternal death (WHO/CEMD definition): occurs during pregnancy or within 42 days of its end. Excludes accidental/incidental deaths. Includes both direct (obstetric cause) and indirect (pre-existing disease aggravated by pregnancy) deaths.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Indirect maternal death: death from a non-obstetric condition aggravated by pregnancy (e.g., cardiac disease, epilepsy, diabetes). Distinct from direct maternal death (complications of pregnancy itself, e.g., haemorrhage, pre-eclampsia, sepsis).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Perinatal mortality rate = (stillbirths + early neonatal deaths) ÷ (live births + stillbirths) × 1000. 'Early neonatal' = deaths in first 7 days of life. This is used as a measure of quality of antenatal and intrapartum care.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Stillbirth (UK definition, registration threshold): ≥24 weeks gestation. However, this question uses CESDI/CMACE reporting criteria for late miscarriage/early stillbirth between 20+0 and 23+6 weeks (or ≥300 g if gestation uncertain). Legal stillbirth registration in the UK is ≥24 completed weeks.

questions.keyPoint: Confidential Enquiry memory: Direct = Due to obstetric cause (haemorrhage, PE, pre-eclampsia, sepsis). Indirect = pre-existing disease aggravated by pregnancy (cardiac disease now leading indirect cause). Coincidental = unrelated to pregnancy.

questions.stem: The structure that gives rise to the uterus.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Paramesonephric (Müllerian) ducts: in females (no MIF/AMH), these develop into the uterine tubes, uterus, cervix, and upper vagina. In males, AMH from Sertoli cells causes regression. Mesonephric (Wolffian) ducts become the male reproductive tract.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: The primitive streak appears in the epiblast at day 14–15, defining the cranial-caudal (head-tail) axis, bilateral symmetry, and the three germ layers. It is the site of gastrulation — cells migrate inwards through the streak to form mesoderm and endoderm.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Mesoderm gives rise to somites (which form vertebrae, skeletal muscle, and dermis), heart, blood vessels, kidneys, gonads, and connective tissue. The paraxial mesoderm specifically forms somites by day 20–30 of development.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Endoderm forms the epithelial lining of the gastrointestinal tract, respiratory tract, liver, pancreas, thyroid, parathyroid glands, and urinary bladder. Endoderm is the innermost of the three primary germ layers.

questions.keyPoint: Germ layer derivatives: Ectoderm = skin, nervous system, sense organs. Mesoderm = muscle, bone, heart, kidneys, gonads, blood. Endoderm = gut lining, liver, pancreas, lungs, thyroid. 'Ecto = Outside, Endo = Inside, Meso = Middle'.

questions.topic: Physiological changes: uterus and cervix (Ten Teachers Ch.5 EMQ)
questions.optionsTitle: Physiological changes: uterus and cervix (Ten Teachers Ch.5 EMQ)
questions.stem: A peptide that is used within the triple test.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insulin-like growth factors I and II

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Human chorionic gonadotrophin (hCG): a peptide hormone used in the triple test (along with AFP and unconjugated oestriol) to screen for Down's syndrome. hCG is elevated in Down's syndrome. Produced by syncytiotrophoblast, peaks at 10 weeks.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Human placental lactogen (hPL): produced by syncytiotrophoblast from ~5 weeks, with levels rising progressively. Has anti-insulin effects (diabetogenic), promotes fetal growth, and prepares breasts for lactation. Reflects placental mass.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Corticotrophin-releasing factor (CRF): produced by the myometrium and placenta. Levels rise exponentially in late pregnancy and may play a role in timing of parturition. Also produced by the hypothalamus controlling the HPA axis.

questions.keyPoint: Triple test (15–20 weeks): hCG ↑, AFP ↓, unconjugated oestriol ↓ in Down's syndrome. In trisomy 18 (Edwards'): all three are low. In open neural tube defects: AFP ↑↑ (with normal hCG and oestriol).

questions.optionsTitle: Physiological changes: uterus and cervix (Ten Teachers Ch.5 EMQ)
questions.stem: A substance that is produced by trophoblast from mid-pregnancy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insulin-like growth factors I and II

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: hCG has structural homology with TSH (both share the alpha subunit) and can stimulate thyroid receptors, leading to mild physiological hyperthyroidism in the first trimester. This explains the transient suppression of TSH seen in early pregnancy.

questions.stem: Levels rise through pregnancy due to increased production of placental isoform.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Haematocrit falls in pregnancy due to the dilutional effect of plasma volume expansion (approximately 50% increase in plasma volume vs only 25% increase in red cell mass). This is physiological dilutional anaemia — haemoglobin falls to ~10.5 g/dL threshold for treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Plasma folate concentration falls in pregnancy due to increased demand (fetal growth, increased cell turnover, haemodilution). Folate supplementation (400 mcg/day preconceptually and in first trimester) prevents neural tube defects. Women with previous NTD or on anti-epileptics need 5 mg/day.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Fibrinogen increases by approximately 50% in pregnancy (from ~2.5 to ~4.5 g/L). This, combined with increases in factors VII, VIII, X, and von Willebrand factor, and decreased protein S, creates the physiological hypercoagulable state of pregnancy — protecting against haemorrhage at delivery.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): rises significantly in pregnancy, primarily due to placental ALP isoform production. Liver ALP may also rise slightly. Normal ALP in pregnancy can be up to 3–4× the non-pregnant upper limit of normal — do not mistake for liver disease.

questions.keyPoint: Pregnancy hypercoagulability: Procoagulant factors UP (fibrinogen, VII, VIII, X, vWF), Anticoagulants DOWN (protein S, antithrombin). Fibrinolysis DOWN (PAI-1 ↑). This protects against PPH but creates VTE risk — the leading cause of direct maternal death in the UK.

questions.stem: Increased by 50 per cent in pregnancy, contributing to hypercoagulable state.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Haematocrit falls in pregnancy due to the dilutional effect of plasma volume expansion (approximately 50% increase in plasma volume vs only 25% increase in red cell mass). This is physiological dilutional anaemia — haemoglobin falls to ~10.5 g/dL threshold for treatment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Plasma folate concentration falls in pregnancy due to increased demand (fetal growth, increased cell turnover, haemodilution). Folate supplementation (400 mcg/day preconceptually and in first trimester) prevents neural tube defects. Women with previous NTD or on anti-epileptics need 5 mg/day.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Fibrinogen increases by approximately 50% in pregnancy (from ~2.5 to ~4.5 g/L). This, combined with increases in factors VII, VIII, X, and von Willebrand factor, and decreased protein S, creates the physiological hypercoagulable state of pregnancy — protecting against haemorrhage at delivery.

choices.label: H

questions.topic: NICE guidelines on routine antenatal care
questions.optionsTitle: NICE guidelines on routine antenatal care
questions.stem: Attend for ultrasound to detect structural abnormalities.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Booking visit (ideally by 10 weeks): folic acid advice, lifestyle counselling (smoking, alcohol, diet, exercise), booking blood tests (FBC, blood group, rhesus status, rubella, syphilis, hepatitis B, HIV, haemoglobinopathy screen), urine culture, and blood pressure.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anatomy (anomaly) scan offered at 18–20+6 weeks: screens for major structural anomalies including neural tube defects, cardiac defects, abdominal wall defects, limb abnormalities, and markers of chromosomal anomalies. About 50% of major cardiac defects are detected.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis: offered at 28 weeks (and 34 weeks in some regimes) to Rhesus D-negative women to prevent sensitisation from feto-maternal haemorrhage. Anti-D is also required after sensitising events (APH, amniocentesis, ECV) at any gestation.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Membrane sweeping is offered at 41 weeks to reduce the need for formal induction of labour. Membrane sweep at 40 weeks (especially for nulliparous women) may also be offered. It does not increase infection risk but can cause discomfort and irregular contractions.

questions.keyPoint: NICE antenatal visits (nulliparous): 10, 16, 18–20, 25, 28, 31, 34, 36, 38, 40, 41 weeks = 11 appointments. Multiparous: 10, 16, 18–20, 28, 34, 36, 38, 40, 41 weeks = 7 appointments (fewer as complications less likely).

questions.stem: Used to date pregnancies when booked between 14 and 20/40.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Estimated fetal weight (EFW): calculated using Hadlock formula incorporating BPD, HC, AC, and FL. Plotted on customised growth charts. EFW alone does not diagnose IUGR — must be combined with AC trend, Doppler studies, and liquor volume assessment.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Head circumference (HC) is used for dating pregnancies at 14–20 weeks. Crown-rump length (CRL) is most accurate before 14 weeks. After 20 weeks dating becomes less reliable as biological variation increases. HC is measured in the axial plane at the level of the thalami and cavum septum pellucidum.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: HC/AC ratio: in asymmetrical IUGR (uteroplacental insufficiency), the fetus preferentially shunts blood to the brain ('brain-sparing'), so head growth is relatively preserved while AC (reflecting liver glycogen stores) falls. HC/AC ratio >97th centile indicates asymmetrical IUGR.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Abdominal circumference (AC): reflects fetal liver size and subcutaneous fat. In macrosomia from poorly controlled maternal diabetes, excess glucose crosses the placenta → fetal hyperinsulinaemia → macrosomia, particularly affecting the trunk (AC > HC). Associated with shoulder dystocia risk.

questions.keyPoint: Growth restriction surveillance: Measure AC and EFW every 2–3 weeks. Umbilical artery Doppler: absent end-diastolic flow → admit + corticosteroids. Reversed end-diastolic flow → urgent delivery. Normal Doppler does NOT exclude placental pathology.

questions.stem: Most suitable diagnostic test where a woman wishes to know fetal karyotype as early in the pregnancy as possible.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cordocentesis (fetal blood sampling): samples fetal blood from the umbilical vein for platelet count, blood group, haematocrit, karyotype, or infection. In fetal alloimmune thrombocytopenia, it confirms fetal platelet count and allows intrauterine platelet transfusion. Miscarriage risk 1–2%.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: CVS (chorionic villus sampling): can be performed from 10–13 weeks (earlier than amniocentesis at 15–16 weeks). Provides fetal karyotype, FISH, microarray, and molecular testing. Miscarriage risk ~1–2%. Transcervical or transabdominal approach. Earlier result = earlier decision-making.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Free fetal DNA (cell-free fetal DNA in maternal plasma): can determine fetal sex (for X-linked conditions), fetal rhesus D status, and screen for trisomies 21, 18, 13. Non-invasive. Available from 10 weeks. Does not carry miscarriage risk unlike CVS or amniocentesis.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: None of the above: currently no non-invasive test gives reliable DIAGNOSIS of single gene defects. Free fetal DNA can screen (e.g., achondroplasia, some dominant conditions) but is not yet diagnostic for most single gene disorders. Invasive testing (CVS or amniocentesis) remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis.

questions.keyPoint: Amniocentesis vs CVS: CVS = 10–13 weeks, faster result for karyotype, 1–2% miscarriage risk. Amniocentesis = 15–16 weeks, slightly lower miscarriage risk (~0.5–1%), also provides AFP (NTD screening). Cordocentesis = 18+ weeks, 1–2% risk, used for fetal blood directly.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman in her first pregnancy presents in labour. Her blood pressure is 145/90. Shortly after beginning regular contractions she has a tonic-clonic seizure.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HELLP syndrome: Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets. ALT 70 is elevated. Occurs in setting of pre-eclampsia (previous history in this patient). May present with epigastric pain, malaise, nausea. Can occur without severe hypertension. Treatment: delivery.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Eclampsia: tonic-clonic seizure in a patient with hypertension (and usually proteinuria) without another cause. Management: IV magnesium sulphate (loading dose 4g, maintenance 1g/h), IV antihypertensives (labetalol or hydralazine). Magnesium is anticonvulsant, NOT an antihypertensive.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Gestational hypertension: hypertension (≥140/90) developing after 20 weeks WITHOUT proteinuria or features of end-organ damage. Managed with antihypertensives if BP ≥150/100, regular monitoring. 25% develop pre-eclampsia, so close follow-up is essential.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Placental abruption: sudden severe abdominal pain + PV bleeding + hypertension. The uterus may be hard/woody (retro-placental clot). May be concealed (no visible bleeding). Major cause of DIC. Emergency management: resuscitation, continuous fetal monitoring, consider delivery.

questions.keyPoint: Pre-eclampsia diagnosis (ISSHP 2018): hypertension ≥140/90 after 20 weeks PLUS one of: proteinuria (>300 mg/24h), maternal organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, neurological, haematological), OR uteroplacental dysfunction. Proteinuria alone is no longer required.

questions.stem: Risk of preterm labour is primarily due to uterine over-distension.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Smoking: independent risk factor for preterm labour, IUGR, placental abruption, and SIDS. Smoking cessation programmes offered at booking and throughout pregnancy. Nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) is safer than continued smoking in pregnancy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Appendicitis: one of the most common surgical emergencies in pregnancy. Perforation risk is higher in pregnancy as the appendix is displaced upward. Surgery required → peritoneal irritation, uterine handling, and inflammatory cytokines all increase preterm labour risk.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Intrauterine bleeding: antepartum haemorrhage, including threatened miscarriage with bleeding in first trimester, is associated with subsequent preterm labour. Blood may act as a uterine irritant, and bleeding may indicate placenta praevia or abruption — both independent PTL risk factors.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Multiple pregnancy: the primary mechanism is uterine over-distension, which activates stretch-sensitive pathways in the myometrium, triggering early labour. Multiple pregnancies carry 5–10× higher risk of preterm birth. Monochorionic twins also at risk from TTTS.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Appendicitis: one of the most common surgical emergencies in pregnancy. Perforation risk is higher in pregnancy as the appendix is displaced upward. Surgery required → peritoneal irritation, uterine handling, and inflammatory cytokines all increase preterm labour risk.

questions.topic: Diagnosis and management of preterm delivery
questions.optionsTitle: Diagnosis and management of preterm delivery
questions.stem: High negative predictive value for detecting preterm pre-labour rupture of the membranes.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Nitrazine test (Amnio-Sure/PAMG-1 now preferred): nitrazine paper turns blue in the presence of amniotic fluid (pH 7.0–7.5) vs normal vaginal secretions (pH 3.8–4.5). Very high NPV (97–98%) for PPROM — a negative test makes PPROM very unlikely. Avoid if there is obvious pooling on speculum.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Cervical cerclage: surgical suture placed around the cervix to prevent mid-trimester pregnancy loss in women with cervical weakness. Contraindicated in: active bleeding, active contractions (may cause rupture), active infection (chorioamnionitis). Also contraindicated in lethal fetal anomaly.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Amniocentesis for suspected chorioamnionitis: amniotic fluid sent for MC&S, glucose level (<3.3 mmol/L suggests infection), and white cell count. Indicated when there is diagnostic uncertainty about intra-amniotic infection, especially in preterm PPROM, before initiating tocolysis or steroids.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Tocolysis: does not improve perinatal outcomes if used long-term, but provides a 48-hour window to administer corticosteroids (betamethasone) and arrange intrauterine transfer to a unit with neonatal intensive care. Nifedipine (calcium channel blocker) or atosiban (oxytocin receptor antagonist) are first-line tocolytics.

questions.keyPoint: Corticosteroids in preterm labour: betamethasone 12mg IM × 2 doses 24h apart (or dexamethasone). Benefit: reduces RDS (by 50%), IVH, NEC. Given 24–34+6 weeks gestation. Consider repeat course if first course given >7 days earlier and <34 weeks. Best evidence 24h–7 days after first dose.

questions.stem: A woman attends for booking at 6 weeks of pregnancy. She has had a previous metallic mitral valve replacement.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Metallic heart valve: requires lifelong anticoagulation (warfarin normally). During pregnancy, warfarin is teratogenic in first trimester (warfarin embryopathy) but necessary for metallic valves. Options: heparin throughout pregnancy, or warfarin after 12 weeks. This requires haematology/cardiology input — warfarin cannot simply be stopped.

choices.label: D
choices.text: 6 weeks postnatal low molecular weight heparin
choices.explanation: Previous VTE + Caesarean section: RCOG recommends 6 weeks of postnatal LMWH. Emergency CS is an additional risk factor. Previous pregnancy-related DVT alone warrants antenatal prophylactic LMWH in subsequent pregnancies plus 6 weeks postnatal.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Discussion with haematologist for expert advice

choices.label: F
choices.text: 1 week post-natal low molecular weight heparin
choices.explanation: Age ≥35 + parity ≥3 + vaginal delivery: RCOG risk score of ≥2 (age 35+ = 1 point, parity 4 = 1 point). Total score ≥2 warrants 7 days of postnatal LMWH. Compare with the 30-year-old above who scores lower. Individualised risk scoring is key.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Para 4 (grand multipara), normal vaginal delivery, healthy: early mobilisation and hydration is the appropriate intervention. She has elevated parity as a VTE risk factor, but with no other risk factors, she does not require pharmacological thromboprophylaxis. RCOG score guides this decision.

choices.label: H
choices.text: Antenatal prophylaxis with low molecular weight heparin

questions.keyPoint: RCOG VTE risk scoring: Previous VTE (+4), thrombophilia (+3), BMI>40 (+2), Caesarean (+1), Age>35 (+1), parity>3 (+1), smoker (+1). Score ≥4 antenatally = prophylactic LMWH throughout. Score ≥2 postnatally = 10 days LMWH. Score ≥3 postnatally = 6 weeks LMWH.

questions.stem: Should be considered abnormal if lasting more than 30 minutes.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Latent phase: onset of regular contractions to 3–4 cm cervical dilatation. Duration is highly variable (may be prolonged, especially in nulliparous women). First trimigravidae may spend many hours in latent phase. Admission in active labour (≥4 cm + regular contractions) is recommended.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Third stage of labour (delivery of placenta): normally lasts up to 30 minutes with active management (oxytocin + controlled cord traction) or up to 60 minutes with physiological management. >30 minutes with active management = retained placenta requiring manual removal under anaesthesia.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Effacement (cervical ripening/shortening): occurs before dilatation in primigravidae (effacement then dilatation) but can occur simultaneously in multigravidae. The cervix shortens from ~3–4 cm to paper-thin as it is taken up into the lower uterine segment.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Active second stage: from full dilatation with active pushing. NICE guideline: no more than 2 hours active pushing in primiparous women (3 hours if epidural) before considering instrumental delivery. Multiparous: 1 hour (2 hours with epidural). Fetal condition guides timing.

questions.keyPoint: Stages of labour: First stage = onset to full dilatation (latent 0–4cm + active 4–10cm). Second stage = full dilatation to delivery of baby (passive + active pushing phases). Third stage = delivery of baby to delivery of placenta. Active management of third stage (oxytocin) reduces PPH by 60%.

questions.stem: After the head delivers through the vulva, it immediately aligns with the fetal shoulders.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Extension: once the occiput reaches the symphysis pubis, the fetal head extends around the symphysis (acting as a fulcrum). The brow, nose, mouth, then chin are born sequentially. This is the reverse of flexion that occurred during engagement and descent.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Engagement: when the widest part of the fetal head (biparietal diameter) has passed through the pelvic inlet (plane of the pelvic brim). In cephalic presentation, engagement means the head is at 0/5 palpable abdominally (0 fifths above the brim). Usually occurs at 36–38 weeks in nulliparous women.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Restitution: after the head is delivered, it rotates back to its original relationship with the shoulders (typically rotating 45° back to the oblique position). This must be distinguished from external rotation, which follows and aligns head with shoulders for delivery of the shoulders.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: None of the above: this describes delivery of the shoulders. After restitution and external rotation, downward traction delivers the anterior shoulder under the symphysis first, then upward traction delivers the posterior shoulder. This step is not named in the standard list of the 'mechanism of labour' cardinal movements.

questions.keyPoint: Cardinal movements of labour (FEDFIRE): Flexion → Engagement → Descent → Internal rotation → Flexion maximum → delivery by Extension → Restitution → External rotation (shoulders align). 'FEDFIRE' — remember the sequence for your exam.

questions.stem: When the widest part of the presenting part has passed successfully through the pelvic inlet.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Extension: once the occiput reaches the symphysis pubis, the fetal head extends around the symphysis (acting as a fulcrum). The brow, nose, mouth, then chin are born sequentially. This is the reverse of flexion that occurred during engagement and descent.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Engagement: when the widest part of the fetal head (biparietal diameter) has passed through the pelvic inlet (plane of the pelvic brim). In cephalic presentation, engagement means the head is at 0/5 palpable abdominally (0 fifths above the brim). Usually occurs at 36–38 weeks in nulliparous women.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Restitution: after the head is delivered, it rotates back to its original relationship with the shoulders (typically rotating 45° back to the oblique position). This must be distinguished from external rotation, which follows and aligns head with shoulders for delivery of the shoulders.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: None of the above: this describes delivery of the shoulders. After restitution and external rotation, downward traction delivers the anterior shoulder under the symphysis first, then upward traction delivers the posterior shoulder. This step is not named in the standard list of the 'mechanism of labour' cardinal movements.

questions.keyPoint: Cardinal movements of labour (FEDFIRE): Flexion → Engagement → Descent → Internal rotation → Flexion maximum → delivery by Extension → Restitution → External rotation (shoulders align). 'FEDFIRE' — remember the sequence for your exam.

questions.stem: A primigravida in spontaneous labour at 34+3/40 has a pathological CTG trace in the second stage. The fetal head is at +2 station and is occipito-anterior.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Brow presentation: the largest diameter (mentovertical ~13.5 cm) presents — cannot deliver vaginally in most cases. Requires Caesarean section. May convert to vertex (with flexion) or face (with extension) spontaneously, but if confirmed brow in active second stage, CS is required.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Kielland's rotational forceps: indicated for occipito-transverse (OT) position at 0 station (at the ischial spines). Kielland's can rotate the head from OT to OA before traction. Only performed by trained operators. Ventouse can also rotate but at 0 station with OT, Kielland's is most appropriate in this scenario.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Kiwi Omnicup: disposable ventouse suitable for OA position at +2 station (below spines) — appropriate for urgent delivery for fetal distress. Preterm (<36 weeks) ventouse is relatively contraindicated due to cephalhaematoma risk, but at 34+3 the clinical urgency and OA position with low station makes ventouse appropriate. Metal cup has higher traction success; Kiwi is suitable for straightforward cases.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Neville Barnes (or Haig Ferguson) forceps: used for OT position at +2 station. After 2 hours of active pushing at a low station, the head needs rotation and traction — straight (non-rotational) forceps like Neville Barnes can be used if the head can be manually rotated first, or Kielland's for direct rotation. Neville Barnes is the typical 'straight' outlet forceps answer here.

questions.keyPoint: Ventouse vs Forceps: Ventouse = less maternal trauma, more neonatal scalp injury (cephalhaematoma, chignon). Forceps = more maternal perineal trauma, better control of fetal head. Ventouse CONTRAINDICATED: <34 weeks, face presentation, fetal bleeding disorder, after fetal blood sampling.

questions.stem: Longitudinal lie where the presenting part is a foot.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Transverse lie: the fetus lies at right angles to the maternal long axis. The presenting part is the shoulder. Vaginal delivery is impossible. At term, attempt ECV, but if unsuccessful → Caesarean section. Common with placenta praevia, polyhydramnios, grand multiparity.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Footling breech: one or both feet present first. Highest risk type of breech — cord prolapse is a major risk. Vaginal delivery is NOT recommended for footling breech. Constitutes an indication for elective Caesarean section.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cephalic: ECV aims to convert a breech (or other malpresentation) to a vertex/cephalic presentation to allow planned vaginal delivery or reduce CS rate. Success rate ~50% overall, higher in multiparous women. Performed from 36 weeks (37 weeks for nulliparous women per RCOG).

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Unstable lie: the lie changes repeatedly (transverse, oblique, cephalic, breech) after 36 weeks. Risk of cord prolapse with SROM. RCOG recommends admission from 37–38 weeks for monitoring. If membranes rupture, emergency assessment for cord prolapse is needed.

questions.keyPoint: Frank/extended breech = hips flexed, knees extended (bottom down, legs up). Complete/flexed breech = hips and knees flexed (fetus 'sitting cross-legged'). Footling = one or both feet below buttocks. Frank breech is the most common type (65%) and most amenable to vaginal breech delivery.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman in her second pregnancy has delivered a live male infant. She has no past medical history of note. Ten minutes after delivery, she complains of sudden onset severe occipital headache associated with vomiting. Shortly after she loses consciousness and is unresponsive to any stimuli.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Subarachnoid haemorrhage: 'thunderclap' headache — sudden, severe, worst-ever headache. Post-delivery collapse with occipital headache and loss of consciousness. Cause: rupture of intracranial aneurysm or AVM. CT head → lumbar puncture (xanthochromia). Neurosurgical emergency.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Pulmonary embolism: the leading cause of direct maternal death in the UK. Post-CS (major VTE risk factor) → sudden breathlessness + chest pain → collapse. Resuscitation first, then CTPA/V-Q scan. Treatment: therapeutic LMWH (or thrombolysis in haemodynamically unstable PE).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Eclampsia: hypertension (120/90) + proteinuria (2+) + seizure in a pregnant woman without other cause. Note the 28-week size fundus at 32 weeks suggests growth restriction. Management: IV magnesium sulphate (prevent further seizures), IV antihypertensives, delivery planning.

questions.keyPoint: Obstetric collapse — think HAEMORRHAGE first (PPH, abruption, ruptured uterus), then ECLAMPSIA, then ANAPHYLAXIS, then AMNIOTIC FLUID EMBOLISM (rare, catastrophic: sudden collapse + coagulopathy + cardiovascular collapse), then PE. Call CRASH TEAM immediately.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman who is 32 weeks pregnant presents to delivery suite. She complains of feeling generally unwell. Clinical examination reveals a 28-week size fetus. Her blood pressure was noted to be 120/90 mmHg and on urine analysis 2+ protein was present. During the clinical examination, she has a seizure.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old gestational diabetic with a BMI of 35 is induced at 42/40. After a long labour, the obstetric registrar plans to deliver with forceps.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cord prolapse: occurs when the presenting part does not fill the lower segment — risk factors include unstable lie, footling breech, PPROM, prematurity. SROM + footling breech = high cord prolapse risk. Management: immediate head-up/knee-chest position, manual elevation of presenting part, emergency Caesarean.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Uterine inversion: failure to deliver the placenta with acute abdominal pain + haemorrhage + feeling of 'something coming down' (uterus inverts through the cervix). Treatment: manual replacement immediately (before cervical ring contracts), IV oxytocin after replacement. Avoid manual placenta removal before replacement.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Uterine rupture: previous uterine scar (CS) + oxytocin augmentation = risk factors. Presents with sudden severe abdominal pain, fetal distress, loss of uterine contractions, maternal collapse. Emergency laparotomy required. VBAC consent should include discussion of 0.5% uterine rupture risk.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Shoulder dystocia: risk factors include macrosomia (GDM + BMI 35 + post-dates), instrumental delivery, prolonged labour. The anterior shoulder impacts behind the symphysis pubis. Management: McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflex thighs), suprapubic pressure, episiotomy, internal manoeuvres (Rubin, Woods' screw), Zavanelli.

questions.keyPoint: Shoulder dystocia HELPERR mnemonic: H=Call for Help, E=Evaluate for Episiotomy, L=Legs (McRoberts), P=Suprapubic Pressure, E=Enter (internal rotational manoeuvres), R=Remove the posterior arm, R=Roll the patient (all-fours). Do NOT apply fundal pressure (worsens impaction).

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is admitted from home. She had a normal vaginal delivery 4 days previously. She presented with heavy, fresh vaginal bleeding and clots. On examination, she has a temperature of 38.3°C. Abdominal examination reveals mild suprapubic tenderness. Vaginal examination revealed blood clots and the cervix admits a finger and is enlarged and bulky.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Chest infection/pneumonia: post-GA Caesarean → aspiration risk, atelectasis, and respiratory infections. Productive cough, elevated RR, accessory muscle use, and coarse crackles = consolidation/pneumonia. Treatment: antibiotics (amoxicillin ± clarithromycin), chest physiotherapy, adequate analgesia.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Endometritis: post-Caesarean section (highest risk), offensive lochia, uterine tenderness, fever. Causative organisms: polymicrobial (GBS, E. coli, anaerobes, Chlamydia). Treatment: broad-spectrum antibiotics IV (e.g., co-amoxiclav + metronidazole). CS carries 5–20× higher endometritis risk vs VD.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Retained products of conception (RPOC): day 4 post-delivery, heavy PV bleeding + fever + bulky, open-os uterus. Management: ultrasound to confirm RPOC, antibiotics, then surgical evacuation (ERPC) or medical management (misoprostol). Risk factors: PPH, incomplete placenta at delivery, instrumental delivery.

questions.keyPoint: Causes of postpartum pyrexia (4 Ws in first 10 days): Wind (day 1–2, atelectasis), Water (day 3–5, UTI), Wound (day 5–7, CS wound/episiotomy infection), Walking (day 5+, DVT/PE). Add: 'Womb' (endometritis, RPOC) and 'Wet nurse' (mastitis, day 3–5 engorgement).

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman is admitted 7 days after having a Caesarean section. She is generally unwell and complains of a foul-smelling vaginal discharge. On examination, she has a temperature of 39.0°C. Abdominal examination reveals suprapubic tenderness. Vaginal examination confirms the offensive discharge and uterine tenderness.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is seen 3 days after having a Caesarean section under general anaesthesia. She is complaining that she is generally unwell and has been coughing up green sputum. On examination, she has a temperature of 38.0°C. Respiratory rate is 30/min and she is using accessory muscles. Chest examination reveals purulent sputum and coarse crackles.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Chest infection/pneumonia: post-GA Caesarean → aspiration risk, atelectasis, and respiratory infections. Productive cough, elevated RR, accessory muscle use, and coarse crackles = consolidation/pneumonia. Treatment: antibiotics (amoxicillin ± clarithromycin), chest physiotherapy, adequate analgesia.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Endometritis: post-Caesarean section (highest risk), offensive lochia, uterine tenderness, fever. Causative organisms: polymicrobial (GBS, E. coli, anaerobes, Chlamydia). Treatment: broad-spectrum antibiotics IV (e.g., co-amoxiclav + metronidazole). CS carries 5–20× higher endometritis risk vs VD.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Retained products of conception (RPOC): day 4 post-delivery, heavy PV bleeding + fever + bulky, open-os uterus. Management: ultrasound to confirm RPOC, antibiotics, then surgical evacuation (ERPC) or medical management (misoprostol). Risk factors: PPH, incomplete placenta at delivery, instrumental delivery.

questions.keyPoint: Causes of postpartum pyrexia (4 Ws in first 10 days): Wind (day 1–2, atelectasis), Water (day 3–5, UTI), Wound (day 5–7, CS wound/episiotomy infection), Walking (day 5+, DVT/PE). Add: 'Womb' (endometritis, RPOC) and 'Wet nurse' (mastitis, day 3–5 engorgement).

questions.stem: At least 30 per cent of women show an improvement in the condition during pregnancy and there is no increased risk of exacerbation postpartum.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Asthma: approximately 1/3 improve, 1/3 stay the same, and 1/3 worsen during pregnancy. Overall, asthma is usually well-controlled in pregnancy. Inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators are safe in pregnancy. Poorly controlled asthma is more harmful than the medications.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cystic fibrosis: management in pregnancy requires multidisciplinary input — nutritional support (high calorie, fat-soluble vitamins), regular physiotherapy, aggressive treatment of respiratory infections, monitoring of lung function (FEV1 <60% predicted = high risk). Women with FEV1 <30% are advised against pregnancy.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Mitral stenosis: the most common acquired cardiac lesion in pregnancy (rheumatic disease). The 50% increase in cardiac output and blood volume causes increased transmitral gradient → elevated left atrial pressure → pulmonary oedema, especially in third trimester. Most women deteriorate during pregnancy. Rate control (beta-blocker) is key.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Pulmonary hypertension (PH): maternal mortality 30–50% in pregnancy. Pregnancy is contraindicated due to inability to increase cardiac output through the fixed pulmonary vascular resistance, leading to right heart failure. Termination should be offered. If pregnancy continues, requires specialist MDT care in a tertiary centre.

questions.keyPoint: Cardiac disease in pregnancy — WHO classification guides risk: WHO I (no increased risk), WHO II (mild-moderate risk), WHO III (significant risk — consider termination), WHO IV (pregnancy contraindicated). Pulmonary hypertension and Eisenmenger's are WHO IV (mortality 30–50%).

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman presents at 33 weeks in her first pregnancy. She is complaining of generalised itching, worse on the palms of her hands and soles of her feet. Blood investigations reveal that she has increased bile acids.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: PPROM (preterm premature rupture of membranes): erythromycin is recommended (ORACLE trial) — reduces infection risk, prolongs pregnancy, and reduces neonatal morbidity. 250mg erythromycin QDS for 10 days (or until delivery). Co-amoxiclav should be avoided (increased risk of neonatal NEC).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Obstetric cholestasis (intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy): raised bile acids + pruritus (palms/soles), ± raised ALT. Treatment: ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) — improves pruritus and may reduce fetal risk. Monitor liver function and bile acids. Delivery at 37 weeks due to risk of stillbirth. Avoid fat-soluble vitamin K deficiency.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Eclampsia with severe pre-eclampsia: magnesium sulphate is the treatment of choice for eclamptic seizures and for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia. MAGPIE trial evidence. Loading dose 4g IV over 5–15 min, maintenance 1g/h. Antidote: calcium gluconate. Also give antihypertensives.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Gestational diabetes with iron-deficiency anaemia: haemoglobin 11 g/dL in a symptomatic Asian woman (high-risk group) requires iron supplementation. The glycosuria and abnormal OGTT (fasting glucose 8.1 mmol/L) confirm GDM — treated with diet ± metformin ± insulin, not the iron supplement. The question asks about the tiredness/lethargy/Hb 11.

questions.keyPoint: PPROM management mnemonic: ABCDE — Antibiotics (erythromycin 10 days), Betamethasone (if <34 weeks), Cervical cultures (swabs), Doppler (fetal), Expectant management with regular monitoring. GBS swab. Delivery at 34 weeks in most units, earlier if infection, fetal distress, or abruption.

questions.stem: Delivery by elective Caesarean section may decrease transmission rate.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HIV: elective CS reduces vertical transmission when maternal viral load is detectable (>400 copies/mL). With undetectable viral load on HAART, vaginal delivery is safe. Three interventions reduce transmission: HAART, elective CS (if VL >400), avoiding breastfeeding. Overall transmission <2% with optimal management.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Varicella zoster (chickenpox): approximately 90% of UK adults are immune (prior infection or vaccination). Susceptible pregnant women exposed to chickenpox should receive VZIG within 10 days. Primary varicella in pregnancy carries risks of varicella pneumonia (maternal), and congenital varicella syndrome (1st/2nd trimester).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Varicella zoster (chickenpox): approximately 90% of UK adults are immune (prior infection or vaccination). Susceptible pregnant women exposed to chickenpox should receive VZIG within 10 days. Primary varicella in pregnancy carries risks of varicella pneumonia (maternal), and congenital varicella syndrome (1st/2nd trimester).

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Rubella: live attenuated vaccine is contraindicated in pregnancy. Non-immune women are offered MMR vaccination postpartum (before discharge). Congenital rubella syndrome (deafness, cataract, cardiac defects, IUGR) is entirely preventable with vaccination. Screen at booking; vaccinate if non-immune after delivery.

questions.keyPoint: TORCH infections in pregnancy: Toxoplasmosis, Other (syphilis, HIV, parvovirus B19, VZV), Rubella, CMV, Herpes. CMV is the most common congenital infection (0.5–2% of births). Parvovirus B19 causes hydrops fetalis (fetal anaemia → high-output cardiac failure). All detected by maternal serology.

questions.stem: Immunity is 90 per cent in the UK adult population.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HIV: elective CS reduces vertical transmission when maternal viral load is detectable (>400 copies/mL). With undetectable viral load on HAART, vaginal delivery is safe. Primary varicella in pregnancy carries risks of varicella pneumonia (maternal), and congenital varicella syndrome (1st/2nd trimester).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Treponema pallidum (syphilis): treatment with benzylpenicillin can provoke a Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, rigors, hypotension from cytokine release as spirochaetes are killed). Warn the patient and monitor in hospital if at risk. Syphilis screening is routine at booking in the UK.

questions.topic: Psychiatric disorders in pregnancy and the puerperium
questions.optionsTitle: Psychiatric disorders in pregnancy and the puerperium
questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents on the fifth day after a normal delivery. Her husband has brought her in to accident and emergency, after he noticed an abrupt change in her behaviour. He describes her as confused, restless and is expressing thoughts of self-harm.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Baby blues: days 1–5 postpartum, self-limiting emotional lability, tearfulness, irritability. Affects up to 80% of women. Emergency CS can be emotionally traumatic, compounding the normal hormonal changes (oestrogen/progesterone fall) that trigger baby blues. Reassurance and support are all that is needed.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Puerperal psychosis: sudden onset, typically days 2–7 postpartum. Presents with confusion, agitation, hallucinations, delusions, mood lability, and thought disorder. Psychiatric emergency — risk of harm to self or baby. Requires inpatient psychiatric admission (mother and baby unit if possible). High recurrence risk.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Bipolar affective disorder: lithium + carbamazepine are mood stabilisers used in bipolar disorder. Both are teratogenic (carbamazepine → neural tube defects; lithium → Ebstein's anomaly of the heart). High-risk pregnancy requiring consultant-led care, psychiatric input, and careful risk-benefit analysis of medication.

questions.keyPoint: Postnatal mental health timeline: Baby blues = days 3–5 (self-limiting, 80% women). PND = weeks 2–8, affects 10–15%, Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) screens (score >12 = likely depression). Puerperal psychosis = days 2–7 (1–2 per 1000 births), psychiatric emergency.

questions.topic: Psychiatric disorders in pregnancy and the puerperium
questions.optionsTitle: Psychiatric disorders in pregnancy and the puerperium
questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman, who had a normal delivery 12 hours earlier, is noted by the ward staff to be having difficulties sleeping, is overactive and expresses feelings of excitement.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Baby blues: affects ~50–80% of women, onset days 3–5 (peaks when milk 'comes in'), characterised by tearfulness, irritability, mood lability, overactivity, anxiety. Self-limiting — resolves within 2 weeks. Reassurance and support. If persists beyond 2 weeks → screen for postnatal depression.

choices.label: E

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman who had an emergency Caesarean section 2 days earlier is noted by the midwives on the ward to be having sleeping difficulties, and is tearful and short-tempered.

choices.label: A

questions.stem: A newborn baby of 36 weeks' gestation, present with cyanosis, tachypnoea, grunting and recession.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Erythema toxicum neonatorum: benign, self-limiting rash in 30–70% of term neonates. Appears days 1–4. Erythematous macules/papules/pustules with white or yellow centres, on the trunk and extremities (not palms/soles). Histology: eosinophils in follicles. Reassure parents — no treatment required.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Klumpke's palsy: injury to lower brachial plexus (C8-T1). Results from excessive traction on an abducted arm (vaginal breech delivery). Presents with 'claw hand' (intrinsic hand muscles paralysed, finger flexors intact), wrist drop, and may have Horner's syndrome. Hand grasp is LOST. Contrast with Erb's (C5-C6) where grasp is preserved.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS / hyaline membrane disease): surfactant deficiency in a preterm neonate (36 weeks, late preterm). Cyanosis, tachypnoea, expiratory grunting (auto-PEEP), and intercostal/subcostal recession (chest wall compliance > lung compliance). CXR: ground-glass appearance. Treatment: surfactant, CPAP/mechanical ventilation.

questions.keyPoint: Neonatal skin conditions: Erythema toxicum (benign, eosinophils, day 1–4) vs Milia (keratin cysts, tiny white papules on nose/cheeks, resolve in weeks) vs Milaria (sweat duct obstruction, 'heat rash', clear vesicles) vs Pustular melanosis (pigmented macules in dark-skinned neonates, benign).

questions.stem: In a newborn postnatal check of a term baby delivered by vaginal breech, the attending senior house officer notes that there is a claw hand with inability to extend the fingers.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Erythema toxicum neonatorum: benign, self-limiting rash in 30–70% of term neonates. Appears days 1–4. Erythematous macules/papules/pustules with white or yellow centres, on the trunk and extremities (not palms/soles). Histology: eosinophils in follicles. Reassure parents — no treatment required.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Klumpke's palsy: injury to lower brachial plexus (C8-T1). Results from excessive traction on an abducted arm (vaginal breech delivery). Presents with 'claw hand' (intrinsic hand muscles paralysed, finger flexors intact), wrist drop, and may have Horner's syndrome. Hand grasp is LOST. Contrast with Erb's (C5-C6) where grasp is preserved.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS / hyaline membrane disease): surfactant deficiency in a preterm neonate (36 weeks, late preterm). Cyanosis, tachypnoea, expiratory grunting (auto-PEEP), and intercostal/subcostal recession (chest wall compliance > lung compliance). CXR: ground-glass appearance. Treatment: surfactant, CPAP/mechanical ventilation.

questions.topic: Normal fetal development: the urinary tract
questions.optionsTitle: Normal fetal development: the urinary tract
questions.stem: Originates on either side of the embryonic midline on the nephrogenic ridge.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Ureteric bud: outgrowth from the mesonephric duct at week 5, branches to form the collecting duct system (collecting tubules, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter). Reciprocal induction with metanephric mesenchyme triggers kidney development. Abnormal ureteric bud branching causes congenital renal anomalies.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Mesoderm (specifically intermediate mesoderm/metanephric mesenchyme): gives rise to the renal parenchyma — nephrons, glomeruli, proximal and distal tubules. The ureteric bud (from mesonephric duct) induces the metanephric mesenchyme to differentiate into nephrons. Collecting system = ureteric bud derivative.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Renal agenesis (bilateral/Potter sequence): absence of kidneys → no fetal urine production → oligohydramnios/anhydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia (lethal), Potter's facies (compressed features), limb deformities. Bilateral renal agenesis is incompatible with life (pulmonary hypoplasia). Unilateral is compatible with normal life.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Pronephros: the first and most primitive kidney, appearing at week 4 on the nephrogenic ridge (intermediate mesoderm). It is non-functional in humans and regresses, but its duct (pronephric duct) is taken over by the mesonephros. The sequence is pronephros → mesonephros → metanephros (permanent kidney).

questions.keyPoint: Kidney development sequence: Pronephros (week 4, non-functional) → Mesonephros (weeks 4–8, transient function) → Metanephros (permanent kidney, week 5 onward). Metanephros = ureteric bud + metanephric mesenchyme interaction. The horseshoe kidney (1:400) results from fusion of lower poles, often trapping in front of IMA.
