questions.stem: Develop(s) into the lower portion of the vagina.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Sinovaginal bulbs: proliferations from the urogenital sinus that fuse to form the vaginal plate, which canalises to form the lower 4/5 of the vagina. The upper 1/5 of the vagina derives from the fused paramesonephric (Müllerian) ducts. Failure of canalisation → vaginal atresia.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Genital folds (urethral folds): in females (without DHT), remain unfused and become the labia minora. In males, DHT causes fusion to form the penile urethra. The genital tubercle becomes the clitoris (female) or glans penis (male). Genital swellings → labia majora (female) or scrotum (male).

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Genital swellings (labioscrotal swellings): in females, remain separate and become the labia majora. In males, DHT causes fusion to form the scrotum. The testes subsequently descend into the scrotum. Failure of testicular descent (cryptorchidism) increases testicular cancer risk.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Genital ridge (gonadal ridge): bilateral thickenings of intermediate mesoderm, present in both sexes. In the presence of two X chromosomes (and absence of SRY/TDF), the medulla regresses and the cortex develops into the ovary. Primordial germ cells migrate from the yolk sac into the genital ridges.

questions.keyPoint: Female genital development (without DHT): Genital tubercle → clitoris. Urethral folds → labia minora (remain unfused). Labioscrotal swellings → labia majora. Müllerian ducts → uterus, tubes, upper vagina. Sinovaginal bulbs → lower vagina. Mesonephric (Wolffian) ducts → regress (Gartner's duct remnant).

questions.stem: Lymphatic drainage of the ovary.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Left renal vein: the left ovarian vein drains into the left renal vein (asymmetric drainage). The right ovarian vein drains directly into the inferior vena cava. This explains why varicoceles (dilated pampiniform plexus) are more common on the left in males (same asymmetry for testicular veins).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Superficial inguinal and femoral nodes: the lower third of the vagina and the vulva drain to the inguinal and femoral nodes. This is why vulval carcinoma presents with inguinal lymphadenopathy and inguinal node dissection is part of vulvectomy. The upper vagina/cervix drains to pelvic nodes.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Obturator, internal and external iliac nodes
choices.explanation: Obturator, internal and external iliac nodes: the cervix and upper vagina drain primarily to the parametrial, obturator, internal iliac (hypogastric), and external iliac nodes. In cervical cancer, these are the first nodal stations at risk. Wertheim's hysterectomy removes these nodes.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Paraaortic (lumbar) lymph nodes: the ovary drains along the ovarian vessels (gonadal vessels) to the paraaortic nodes at the level of L1–L2, mirroring the embryological origin of the gonad from the nephrogenic ridge. This is clinically important in ovarian cancer staging and spread.

questions.keyPoint: Lymph node drainage summary: Vulva/lower vagina → inguinal/femoral. Cervix/upper vagina → obturator/external iliac/internal iliac. Uterine body → external iliac/paraaortic. Ovary → paraaortic (L1-L2). Fallopian tube → paraaortic. All can drain to common iliac → paraaortic → thoracic duct.

questions.topic: Disorders of the menstrual cycle — diagnosis
questions.optionsTitle: Disorders of the menstrual cycle — diagnosis
questions.stem: Intermenstrual bleeding.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Adenomyosis: endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium causing dysmenorrhoea and menorrhagia. Uterus is enlarged, boggy, and tender on examination. Ultrasound findings: heterogeneous myometrium, 'Swiss cheese' appearance. Definitive diagnosis on histology. Treatment: Mirena IUS, GnRH analogues, or hysterectomy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Endometrial polyp: common cause of intermenstrual bleeding (IMB) and postcoital bleeding. Diagnosed by transvaginal ultrasound (thickened endometrium, focal area) or hysteroscopy. Treatment: hysteroscopic polypectomy. Also consider: cervical ectropion, cervical/endometrial cancer, PID.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Malignancy of the cervix: postcoital bleeding (PCB) is a classic symptom of cervical cancer and must be investigated urgently (2-week wait referral). Cervical ectropion is a more common benign cause, but cancer must be excluded. Examination + cervical smear + colposcopy if persistent PCB.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Endometrial malignancy: postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) = endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. Approximately 10% of PMB is due to endometrial cancer; 90% from benign causes (atrophic vaginitis, polyps, HRT). Investigation: TVUSS (endometrial thickness >4mm → biopsy), outpatient hysteroscopy + biopsy.

questions.keyPoint: Causes of abnormal uterine bleeding (PALM-COEIN): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia — structural. Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified — functional. Use this framework when clerking any woman with menstrual complaints.

Investigation: TVUSS (endometrial thickness >4mm → biopsy), outpatient hysteroscopy + biopsy.

questions.keyPoint: Causes of abnormal uterine bleeding (PALM-COEIN): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia — structural. Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified — functional. Use this framework when clerking any woman with menstrual complaints.

questions.topic: Disorders of the menstrual cycle — diagnosis
questions.optionsTitle: Disorders of the menstrual cycle — diagnosis
questions.stem: Painful periods.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Adenomyosis: endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium causing dysmenorrhoea and menorrhagia. Uterus is enlarged, boggy, and tender on examination. Ultrasound findings: heterogeneous myometrium, 'Swiss cheese' appearance. Definitive diagnosis on histology. Treatment: Mirena IUS, GnRH analogues, or hysterectomy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Endometrial polyp: common cause of intermenstrual bleeding (IMB) and postcoital bleeding. Diagnosed by transvaginal ultrasound (thickened endometrium, focal area) or hysteroscopy. Treatment: hysteroscopic polypectomy. Also consider: cervical ectropion, cervical/endometrial cancer, PID.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Malignancy of the cervix: postcoital bleeding (PCB) is a classic symptom of cervical cancer and must be investigated urgently (2-week wait referral). Cervical ectropion is a more common benign cause, but cancer must be excluded. Examination + cervical smear + colposcopy if persistent PCB.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Endometrial malignancy: postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) = endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. Approximately 10% of PMB is due to endometrial cancer; 90% from benign causes (atrophic vaginitis, polyps, HRT). Investigation: TVUSS (endometrial thickness >4mm → biopsy), outpatient hysteroscopy + biopsy.

questions.keyPoint: Causes of abnormal uterine bleeding (PALM-COEIN): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia — structural. Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified — functional. Use this framework when clerking any woman with menstrual complaints.

questions.stem: The proliferation of trophoblastic tissue with or without embryonic tissue.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Threatened miscarriage: PV bleeding <24 weeks + closed cervix + fetal cardiac activity seen on USS. 25% of all pregnancies have first trimester bleeding. Management: reassurance + USS. 50% of threatened miscarriages will complete. Progesterone support (PROMISE/PRISM trial evidence) may reduce miscarriage in women with previous loss.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Incomplete miscarriage: products of conception partially expelled, cervix open, bleeding ± pain. USS shows retained POC >15mm (RCOG: >15mm mixed echogenicity = significant, >65mm in this question). Management: expectant, medical (misoprostol), or surgical (ERPC). Antibiotics if infection risk.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Ectopic pregnancy: classic presentation — amenorrhoea + PV spotting/bleeding + unilateral pelvic pain. Shoulder tip pain = diaphragmatic irritation from haemoperitoneum. Empty uterus on USS + fluid in POD = ectopic until proven otherwise. Serum hCG + repeat USS. Management: expectant, methotrexate, or salpingectomy.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Hydatidiform mole (gestational trophoblastic disease): abnormal placental development. Complete mole (no embryonic tissue, diploid 46XX or 46XY all paternal) vs partial mole (triploid, abnormal embryo present). hCG very high. Risk of malignant transformation to choriocarcinoma. Managed with uterine evacuation + hCG monitoring.

questions.keyPoint: Ectopic pregnancy risk factors: Previous ectopic (+7×), previous tubal surgery, PID/Chlamydia, IUCD in situ, ART/IVF, endometriosis, smoking. 95% are tubal (ampullary most common). Cornual/interstitial ectopics are rarer but more dangerous (rupture at larger gestation).

questions.stem: A sexually transmitted disease typified by genital ulcers and painful vesicles.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Candida (thrush): vulvovaginal candidiasis. Not sexually transmitted. White, curdy/cottage-cheese discharge, intense pruritus, vaginal erythema, dyspareunia. Caused by Candida albicans (85%). Treatment: clotrimazole pessary or fluconazole oral. Risk factors: antibiotics, diabetes, pregnancy, immunosuppression.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bacterial vaginosis (BV): not an STI. Overgrowth of anaerobes (Gardnerella, Prevotella) replacing normal Lactobacillus. Thin, homogeneous, grey-white discharge with fishy odour (especially after sex/menstruation). Whiff test positive (10% KOH). Clue cells on microscopy. Amsel criteria (3 of 4). Treatment: metronidazole.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Herpes simplex (HSV-2 mainly, HSV-1 increasingly): primary infection causes painful vesicles → ulcers on the vulva, vagina, cervix, with inguinal lymphadenopathy. Systemic symptoms (fever, malaise). Recurrent episodes less severe. Treatment: aciclovir. Neonatal herpes risk with primary infection at delivery → CS.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae): Gram-negative intracellular diplococci. Infects columnar epithelium (cervix, urethra, rectum, pharynx, conjunctiva). 50% of women are asymptomatic. Complications: PID, tubal infertility, Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis). Treatment: ceftriaxone IM + azithromycin (dual therapy for resistance).

questions.keyPoint: Vaginal discharge diagnosis: BV = fishy smell, clue cells, pH >4.5. Candida = curdy white, no smell, pH normal, pseudohyphae. Trichomonas = frothy yellow-green, strawberry cervix, pH >4.5, motile protozoa. Gonorrhoea = purulent, Gram-negative diplococci. Chlamydia = often asymptomatic, mucopurulent cervicitis.

questions.stem: Detrusor pressure rise >15 cm of water during filling associated with urgency.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Urodynamic stress incontinence (USI, formerly 'genuine stress incontinence'): leakage on raised intra-abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze, exercise) without detrusor contraction on urodynamics. Due to impaired urethral closure mechanism. Treatment: pelvic floor exercises (PFMT), duloxetine, TVT/TOT (mid-urethral sling), colposuspension.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Poor detrusor contraction: low voiding pressure (10 cmH2O) with low flow rate (5 mL/s) = underactive bladder. Symptoms: voiding difficulty, incomplete emptying, overflow incontinence. Causes: denervation, diabetic cystopathy, after radical pelvic surgery. Treatment: clean intermittent self-catheterisation (CISC).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Urethral obstruction: very high voiding pressure (>70 cmH2O) with poor flow rate (5 mL/s) = obstruction at the level of the urethra or bladder neck. In women, can follow anti-incontinence surgery (TVT too tight) or pelvic organ prolapse. Management: urethral dilatation, urethrotomy, or sling revision.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Detrusor overactivity (previously 'detrusor instability'): involuntary detrusor contractions during filling phase of cystometry (>15 cmH2O = significant). Presents with urgency, frequency, nocturia, and urge incontinence (OAB wet). Treatment: bladder training, antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, solifenacin), mirabegron (β3 agonist), botulinum toxin.

questions.keyPoint: Urodynamics key values: Normal detrusor pressure during filling = <15 cmH2O. Normal voiding pressure = 40–60 cmH2O. Normal peak flow rate = >15 mL/s (female). Post-void residual <50 mL = normal. Detrusor overactivity = filling pressure >15 cmH2O with urgency.

questions.stem: 4–8 weeks for uterine involution before utilizing.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP): oestrogen increases VTE risk. COCP is contraindicated for 6 weeks postpartum (all women) due to puerperium being a high VTE risk period, and for 6 months if breastfeeding (suppresses lactation + VTE risk). Use progestogen-only methods (POP, Depo, IUS/IUD) instead.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM): exclusive breastfeeding + amenorrhoea + <6 months postpartum = <2% failure rate. All three criteria must be met. Highly effective in this specific context. If any criterion changes (supplemental feeds, return of menstruation, >6 months), alternative contraception needed.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Laparoscopic clip sterilisation: failure rate 1:200 (lifetime) — lower than most reversible methods long-term. Considered permanent. Filshie clip most commonly used. Counselling must include failure rate, irreversibility, and alternative long-acting reversible contraception (LARC). Not to be done in the puerperium (high failure rate).

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: IUCD (intrauterine contraceptive device): insertion is recommended 4–8 weeks postpartum to allow complete uterine involution. Immediate postpartum insertion (within 48 hours) is possible but has higher expulsion rates. Copper IUD or Mirena IUS. Highly effective (>99%), reversible, and suitable for breastfeeding.

questions.keyPoint: Postpartum contraception timing (UK FPA guidance): Immediate (within 48h or 4 weeks): POP, implant, progestogen injection, IUS/IUD. 3 weeks: COCP safe (if non-breastfeeding, no VTE risk factors). 6 weeks: COCP if breastfeeding. LARC methods (IUS/IUD, implant) are most effective — encourage at 6-week postnatal visit.

LARC methods (IUS/IUD, implant) are most effective — encourage at 6-week postnatal visit.

questions.stem: Increases risk of thromboembolism in the puerperium.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP): oestrogen increases VTE risk. COCP is contraindicated for 6 weeks postpartum (all women) due to puerperium being a high VTE risk period, and for 6 months if breastfeeding (suppresses lactation + VTE risk). Use progestogen-only methods (POP, Depo, IUS/IUD) instead.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM): exclusive breastfeeding + amenorrhoea + <6 months postpartum = <2% failure rate. All three criteria must be met. Highly effective in this specific context. If any criterion changes (supplemental feeds, return of menstruation, >6 months), alternative contraception needed.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Laparoscopic clip sterilisation: failure rate 1:200 (lifetime) — lower than most reversible methods long-term. Considered permanent. Filshie clip most commonly used. Counselling must include failure rate, irreversibility, and alternative long-acting reversible contraception (LARC). Not to be done in the puerperium (high failure rate).

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: IUCD (intrauterine contraceptive device): insertion is recommended 4–8 weeks postpartum to allow complete uterine involution. Immediate postpartum insertion (within 48 hours) is possible but has higher expulsion rates. Copper IUD or Mirena IUS. Highly effective (>99%), reversible, and suitable for breastfeeding.

questions.keyPoint: Postpartum contraception timing (UK FPA guidance): Immediate (within 48h or 4 weeks): POP, implant, progestogen injection, IUS/IUD. 3 weeks: COCP safe (if non-breastfeeding, no VTE risk factors). 6 weeks: COCP if breastfeeding. LARC methods (IUS/IUD, implant) are most effective — encourage at 6-week postnatal visit.
